knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Prioritize the session now, before the knowledge disperses with the team
knowledge_checks.explanation: The capture window closes when the team disperses — afterwards, recollection fades and calendars never align. Success doesn't reduce the value of lessons, a solo memory exercise loses every perspective but one, and 'when the team reassembles' is a polite word for never.

title: Monitoring Performance and Managing Change
learning_objectives: Run the integrated change control loop: request, impact analysis, decision, baseline update, communication.
Use variance thresholds to scale responses to deviations.
Spot the change-control failure patterns: bypass, cumulative drift, and incomplete implementation.

sections.title: The Change Control Loop
sections.content:
Integrated change control is a loop with no skippable steps: a **change request** is documented; its **impact** is analyzed across scope, schedule, cost, quality, and risk; the right **authority** (project manager within delegated limits, change control board above them) decides; approved changes update the **baselines and plans**; and the outcome is **communicated** to everyone affected — including the stakeholder who asked.

Each step exists because of a specific failure: undocumented requests get forgotten or disputed; unanalyzed changes surprise; wrong-level decisions undermine governance; un-updated baselines corrupt every later variance; and uncommunicated outcomes let stale expectations persist.

sections.title: Thresholds Scale the Response
sections.content: Not every deviation deserves the same machinery. **Variance thresholds**, agreed in advance, define which deviations are noise to monitor and which trigger action or escalation. Applying full escalation to trivial variances buries real signals; ignoring threshold breaches defeats the point of measuring. The same logic tiers change control itself — lightweight paths for low-risk changes, rigorous review for high-risk ones — and the tiers only work if changes are routed honestly by their real risk.

sections.title: Failure Patterns
sections.content:
Three patterns dominate exam scenarios. **Bypass**: changes implemented informally — 'small tweaks,' emergency paths used for convenience, verbal approvals — eroding the baseline's meaning. **Cumulative drift**: individually approved small changes whose combined effect nobody assessed; thresholds keyed to single changes miss the aggregate. **Incomplete implementation**: a change approved but its baseline updates, documentation, or timing quietly diverging — approved-but-unreflected changes make future measurement fiction.

The credited response to each: restore the loop, assess the aggregate, and close the paperwork the loop requires.

sections.title: Exam Perspective
sections.content: When new information invalidates an approved change's analysis (a ripple effect, an infeasibility), the credited move returns to the approving authority with updated facts — neither proceeding as approved nor improvising a substitute. And remember who owns what: the PM analyzes and recommends; approval belongs to the level governance assigns.

exam_traps: Implementing 'small' changes informally — size doesn't exempt a change from the loop.
Assessing changes only individually while their cumulative drift goes unmeasured.
Treating an approved change as done without updating baselines and informing the affected.

flashcards.definition: Request → impact analysis → authorized decision → baseline update → communication; no skippable steps.
Pre-agreed deviation limits separating monitoring noise from action and escalation triggers.
The combined effect of many individually small approved changes — needs periodic aggregate review.

knowledge_checks.question: A change was properly approved and implemented, but the schedule and cost baselines were never updated to reflect it. What is the consequence?
options: None — approval is the governance step that matters; baselines are bookkeeping
The change becomes invalid and must therefore be reversed immediately
The change must be re-submitted and re-approved by the board a second time
Future variances get measured against references that no longer reflect the plan
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Future variances get measured against references that no longer reflect the plan
knowledge_checks.explanation: Baselines are the measuring stick; leave them stale after an approved change and all subsequent variance analysis compares actuals to a fiction. The fix is completing the loop — updating the baselines — not reversing or re-approving a legitimately approved change.

knowledge_checks.question: A minor schedule variance well inside the agreed threshold is triggering the same escalation as major variances. What should change?
options: Apply the agreed thresholds so escalation matches the deviation's significance
Nothing — treating all variances identically ensures consistent governance
Stop tracking minor variances entirely to reduce reporting noise
Remove thresholds and escalate everything, since caution is costless
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Apply the agreed thresholds so escalation matches the deviation's significance
knowledge_checks.explanation: Thresholds exist precisely to scale response to significance — escalating noise buries real signals and wastes attention. Uniform escalation isn't consistency, it's the absence of judgment; and minor variances still get monitored, just not escalated.

knowledge_checks.question: During implementation, an approved change turns out to have an unanticipated ripple effect on another system, missed by the original impact analysis. What is the credited path?
options: Proceed exactly as approved — the change already passed formal review
Quietly engineer around the ripple effect without informing anyone
Pause, assess the new impact, and return to the board with updated facts
Reverse the change entirely, since its analysis proved incomplete
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Pause, assess the new impact, and return to the board with updated facts
knowledge_checks.explanation: The approval rested on an impact analysis that's now known to be incomplete — the authority that approved deserves the corrected picture before implementation continues. Proceeding rides an invalidated approval, silent workarounds bypass oversight, and automatic reversal overreacts before the new information is even assessed.

title: Project and Phase Closure
learning_objectives: List the closure activities that make a project genuinely done.
Explain why closure discipline applies equally to cancelled and terminated projects.
Distinguish deliverable acceptance from administrative and contract closure.

sections.title: What Closure Actually Includes
sections.content:
Closure is a work package, not a ceremony: confirm **formal acceptance** of deliverables; complete **contract closure** with vendors (final payments, performance evaluations, liability release); finalize **documentation** — including reconstructing any records, like change logs, that execution left incomplete; capture and file **lessons learned**; **transition** the deliverable to operations with the receiving team genuinely ready; **release resources** properly; and close the books.

Deliverables accepted but contracts open, or records missing, means the project is not closed — it's abandoned in place.

sections.title: Cancelled Projects Close Too
sections.content: Early termination doesn't waive closure. A cancelled project still owes the organization formal closure: documented reasons and state at termination, lessons learned (often the most valuable kind), released resources, closed contracts, and archived records. 'Leave it open in case funding returns' or 'skip closure since it failed' both leave the organization with dangling obligations and lost learning.

sections.title: Transition Readiness
sections.content: Handoff to operations is complete when the receiving team can actually operate — trained people, tooling configured (down to details like ticket categories), documentation usable by *their* audience, and support processes live. Announcing the transition is not the transition. The support gap right after handoff is where unready operations teams meet their first real incidents; verifying readiness before closure is the cheap version of that discovery.

sections.title: Exam Perspective
sections.content: Closure scenarios test completeness under pressure: the team dispersing before lessons learned, sponsors pushing to skip 'formalities,' vendor contracts left open after acceptance, or informal scope-drop decisions never documented. The credited options finish the closure work — proportionately (a lean lessons format beats none), but genuinely.

exam_traps: Treating customer acceptance of deliverables as complete closure while vendor contracts remain open.
Skipping closure for cancelled projects — termination increases, not removes, the need to close properly.
Declaring transition complete when responsibility was announced but the receiving team can't yet operate.

flashcards.definition: Final payments, vendor performance evaluation, and liability release — required before project closure.
Finalizing records, lessons learned, archives, and resource release.
The receiving team's genuine ability to operate the deliverable — verified, not announced.

knowledge_checks.question: Deliverables are accepted, but two vendor contracts still have open final payments and performance evaluations. Can the project close?
options: Yes — customer acceptance is the milestone that defines project completion
Not yet — contract closure is part of project closure, not an optional epilogue
Yes, if procurement agrees to inherit the contracts without PM involvement
Only the sponsor can decide whether open contracts block closure
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Not yet — contract closure is part of project closure, not an optional epilogue
knowledge_checks.explanation: Open contracts are unfinished project obligations — payments, evaluations, and liability release remain the project's to complete. Acceptance is necessary but not sufficient; handing the leftovers to procurement without oversight abandons accountability, and this is a process requirement, not a sponsor preference.

knowledge_checks.question: A project is cancelled by a funding cut before producing any deliverable. What does the organization still need from the project manager?
options: Nothing — closure activities exist to celebrate successfully delivered outcomes
Only the financial books; the other closure steps assume deliverables exist
Formal closure: documented state, lessons learned, released resources, closed contracts
An indefinite pause status maintained in case the funding is later restored
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Formal closure: documented state, lessons learned, released resources, closed contracts
knowledge_checks.explanation: Termination changes the outcome, not the obligations: the organization needs the record, the learning (often richest from cancelled work), its people back, and its contracts settled. Books-only closure leaves everything else dangling, and 'paused indefinitely' is unclosed by another name.

knowledge_checks.question: Support responsibility for a new system was formally announced to operations, but their ticketing system has no category for it and no one is trained. What's the closure-relevant reading?
options: Transition is complete — the formal announcement assigned the responsibility
Any operations problem occurring after handoff belongs to operations by definition
Training for the receiving team is a nice-to-have that shouldn't gate closure
Transition isn't done — verify their readiness to operate before closing
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Transition isn't done — verify their readiness to operate before closing
knowledge_checks.explanation: A transition succeeds when the receiving team can actually operate — announcement without capability leaves a support gap exactly when the first incidents arrive. Blaming operations after handoff ignores that the project controlled the readiness verification, and training for the receiving team is foundational, not cosmetic.

title: Procurement, Contracts, and Sourcing
learning_objectives: Apply make-or-buy analysis with total cost of ownership.
Match contract types to scope certainty and risk allocation.
Manage the procurement life cycle: solicitation, selection, administration, closure.

sections.title: Make or Buy, Honestly Costed
sections.content: The **make-or-buy** decision weighs cost, internal capability and capacity, strategic importance, and risk — never a standing preference for building or buying. Cost comparisons must use **total cost of ownership**: a custom build that looks cheaper up front can carry years of maintenance burden that reverses the economics. Strategic value matters too — capabilities the organization must retain argue for making; commodities argue for buying.

sections.title: Contract Types Allocate Risk
sections.content:
Contract type is a risk-allocation decision keyed to scope certainty. **Fixed-price** suits well-defined, stable scope — the seller carries cost risk, so sellers price vague scope defensively. **Cost-reimbursable** suits uncertain or evolving scope — the buyer carries cost risk in exchange for flexibility. **Time and materials** suits small or staff-augmentation work where defining scope up front isn't worth it. Mechanisms like pricing tiers or incentive fees tune the allocation — a fixed-price contract with volume tiers handles quantity uncertainty without abandoning the fixed-price core.

The recurring dispute generator is a vague **statement of work**: ambiguity about deliverables becomes a fight about what the price includes.

sections.title: Selection and Administration
sections.content:
Bidder selection applies **predefined weighted criteria** — price, capability, timeline, references, risk — not price alone; an unusually low bid is a signal to investigate (misunderstood scope, planned shortcuts, financial distress), and evaluation integrity requires addressing conflicts of interest and treating bidders fairly under shared rules.

Procurement continues after signature: monitor vendor performance against the agreement, document deviations like missed milestones formally, and use renewals as the natural point to renegotiate terms the current contract got wrong (loose SLAs, missing metrics). Legitimate leverage comes from the contract and the record — which is why both need maintaining.

sections.title: Exam Perspective
sections.content: Credited options: analysis before commitment (make-or-buy with TCO, weighted selection), matching contract type to scope certainty, formal documentation of vendor issues even when the contract is silent, and constructive engagement (negotiate intent-vs-wording gaps with procurement/legal support) over either capitulation or instant termination.

exam_traps: Comparing build-vs-buy on upfront cost while ignoring years of maintenance burden.
Selecting the lowest bid without investigating why it's so low.
Assuming procurement ends at contract signature — administration and closure remain.

flashcards.definition: Weighing cost (TCO), capability, capacity, and strategic value — not defaulting to build or buy.
Seller carries cost risk; fits well-defined, stable scope.
Buyer carries cost risk; fits uncertain or evolving scope.
The procurement's scope definition — ambiguity here becomes a dispute later.

knowledge_checks.question: Scope for a component is well-defined and stable, but production volumes are genuinely uncertain. Which contracting approach fits?
options: Time and materials, since volume uncertainty makes all scope definition pointless
Cost-reimbursable, to keep maximum flexibility for the buyer throughout
Any type works equally well when the vendor has a strong track record
Fixed-price with a volume mechanism such as pricing tiers
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Fixed-price with a volume mechanism such as pricing tiers
knowledge_checks.explanation: Stable, well-defined scope earns fixed-price economics; the one genuine uncertainty (volume) gets a targeted mechanism rather than abandoning the fixed-price core. Cost-reimbursable shifts all cost risk to the buyer needlessly, T&M answers a scope-definition problem this contract doesn't have, and track record doesn't erase risk-allocation differences.

knowledge_checks.question: One bid comes in dramatically below every competitor for the same scope. What does sound procurement practice require before selecting?
options: Select it — lowest responsive bid wins under standard procurement rules
Investigate the cause — misunderstood scope, planned shortcuts, or vendor distress
Disqualify it automatically, since outlier bids indicate gaming
Ask the low bidder to raise their price to match the field average
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Investigate the cause — misunderstood scope, planned shortcuts, or vendor distress
knowledge_checks.explanation: An outlier price is information: it may be genuine advantage or a misread SOW, quality shortcuts, or a vendor in trouble — each with different consequences for the project. Auto-selecting inherits the unknown cause; auto-disqualifying may discard a legitimately strong bid; price-matching requests manipulate the field instead of understanding it.

knowledge_checks.question: A build-vs-buy comparison favors building based on upfront cost alone. What is the most important missing element?
options: Total cost of ownership — the long maintenance burden custom builds carry
The vendor's published marketing references supporting the buy option
A team vote on which of the two options they would prefer to work on
Legal review of the standard license terms attached to the buy option
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Total cost of ownership — the long maintenance burden custom builds carry
knowledge_checks.explanation: Custom builds carry a long maintenance tail that upfront comparisons systematically miss — TCO is what makes the economics honest. References and license review have their place but don't correct the core analytical gap, and team preference is not a costing input.

title: Requirements Elicitation and Analysis

eco_mappings.task: Task 2: Develop and manage project scope

learning_objectives: Name the major requirement categories and why classification matters.
Match elicitation techniques to the information being sought.
Explain traceability: every requirement linked to its origin and its verification.

sections.title: Requirement Categories
sections.content:
Requirements come in layers, and confusing them causes real defects: **business requirements** (why the organization is doing this — objectives, measurable outcomes), **stakeholder requirements** (what specific groups need), **solution requirements**, split into **functional** (what the product does) and **nonfunctional** (how well — performance, security, usability), **transition requirements** (what's needed to move from old to new — training, migration), and **project requirements** (conditions the project itself must meet, like compliance milestones).

Nonfunctional and transition requirements are the chronically forgotten ones — products that work but can't handle load, and go-lives with no one trained.

sections.title: Elicitation Is Active Work
sections.content:
Requirements are *elicited*, not collected — stakeholders rarely hand over complete, consistent, testable statements. Techniques match the situation: **interviews** for depth from individuals; **workshops** for cross-functional reconciliation (conflicts surface and resolve in the room); **surveys** for breadth at scale; **observation** for what people actually do (which differs from what they say); **prototypes** for reactions to something concrete when words fail; **document analysis** for rules buried in existing systems and regulations.

Ambiguity is the enemy: a requirement two reasonable developers implement contradictorily wasn't a requirement — it was a coin toss. Testable, specific language is the standard.

sections.title: Traceability
sections.content: A **requirements traceability matrix** links each requirement backward to its origin (who needs it, which business objective it serves) and forward to design, build, and test. Traceability answers two questions that otherwise generate disputes: 'why is this requirement here?' (origin) and 'how do we know it's met?' (verification). It also exposes orphans — requirements no objective justifies, and objectives no requirement serves.

sections.title: Exam Perspective
sections.content: Scenario signals: contradictory implementations trace back to ambiguous requirements — clarify and formally update the requirement, don't just pick a winner. Stakeholders insisting on undocumented needs signal elicitation gaps — validate against the documented baseline, route genuinely new needs through change control. And 'the requirements were complete but users hate it' usually means nonfunctional or usability requirements never got captured.

exam_traps: Capturing functional requirements thoroughly while nonfunctional (performance, usability, security) go unstated.
Resolving an ambiguous requirement informally between developers without fixing the documentation.
Treating stakeholder statements as finished requirements without analysis for conflicts, gaps, and testability.

flashcards.definition: How well the product must perform — performance, security, usability, reliability.
What's needed to move from current to future state — training, migration, cutover.
Links each requirement to its origin and its verification — exposing orphans in both directions.

knowledge_checks.question: A delivered system does everything specified but collapses under normal user load. Which requirements category was most likely neglected?
options: Nonfunctional requirements — performance was never specified, so it was never built or tested
Business requirements — the organization's objectives were clearly never documented
Transition requirements — the cutover plan obviously lacked a training component
Project requirements — a compliance milestone must have been missed during execution
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Nonfunctional requirements — performance was never specified, so it was never built or tested
knowledge_checks.explanation: Functionality worked, so functional requirements were captured; what failed is how well the system performs under load — the definition of a nonfunctional gap. Business objectives, training, and compliance milestones don't explain a load collapse.

knowledge_checks.question: Stakeholders from four departments hold conflicting expectations about a shared feature. Which elicitation technique best surfaces and resolves the conflict?
options: Separate structured interviews with each department, documented independently
A facilitated cross-functional workshop where the conflicts surface and reconcile
A broad anonymous survey quantifying each department's stated preference
Observation sessions covering each department's current workflow in isolation
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: A facilitated cross-functional workshop where the conflicts surface and reconcile
knowledge_checks.explanation: Conflicting cross-group expectations need the groups in one room where the conflict becomes visible and negotiable — that's the workshop's specific strength. Separate interviews, surveys, and isolated observation each capture the positions but leave the reconciliation undone.

knowledge_checks.question: Two developers implemented the same documented requirement in contradictory ways; integration testing caught it. Beyond fixing the code, what does good requirements practice demand?
options: Assigning both of the developers to a requirements-writing refresher course
Accepting whichever of the two implementations was finished first as standard
Letting the developers agree verbally on one interpretation and moving on quickly
Clarifying and formally updating the requirement, then verifying both against it
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Clarifying and formally updating the requirement, then verifying both against it
knowledge_checks.explanation: The requirement itself was ambiguous — fix the source, or the same ambiguity bites the next reader. Verbal agreement leaves the documented ambiguity alive; first-finished is arbitrary; and training may help someday but doesn't repair this requirement.

title: Product Scope vs. Project Scope

eco_mappings.task: Task 2: Develop and manage project scope

learning_objectives: Distinguish product scope (the deliverable's features) from project scope (the work to deliver it).
Explain why project scope always exceeds product scope.
Recognize scope creep and gold plating as distinct scope failures.

sections.title: Two Scopes, One Project
sections.content:
**Product scope** is the set of features and functions of the deliverable — what the thing *is*. **Project scope** is all the work required to deliver it — which includes building the product *plus* everything around it: planning, testing, training, documentation, deployment, management itself.

Project scope therefore always exceeds product scope. Estimating only the product work — and forgetting testing, migration, training, coordination — is a classic underestimation pattern.

sections.title: Verification Differs Too
sections.content: The two scopes are verified differently: product scope is measured against the **requirements** (does the product do what was specified?); project scope is measured against the **project management plan** (was all the agreed work performed?). A project can complete its product scope while leaving project scope unfinished — the system works, but nobody was trained and the documentation doesn't exist.

sections.title: Creep and Gold Plating
sections.content:
**Scope creep** is uncontrolled growth — additions that never passed through change control (or backlog prioritization in adaptive work), each 'small tweak' individually harmless and collectively baseline-destroying. **Gold plating** is the team's own contribution: adding unrequested extras 'to delight the customer.' Both are scope failures — gold plating included, because unrequested work spends budget and adds risk for value nobody validated, and the customer now owns features they never asked to maintain.

The discipline is the same for both: scope changes go through the process, whoever proposes them — stakeholder or developer.

sections.title: Exam Perspective
sections.content: Watch for the estimation trap (product work estimated, project work forgotten), the 'it's just a small addition' pattern (creep), and the well-intentioned engineer adding an unrequested improvement (gold plating — credited response: route it through the process, don't quietly ship it). In adaptive contexts, the backlog and definition of done carry the same discipline.

exam_traps: Estimating the product work and forgetting the project work — testing, training, documentation, coordination.
Treating gold plating as generosity — unrequested extras spend budget and add unvalidated risk.
Letting 'small' additions bypass control because each one individually seems harmless.

flashcards.definition: The features and functions of the deliverable — verified against requirements.
All work required to deliver the product — verified against the plan.
Team-added unrequested extras — a scope failure even when well-intentioned.

knowledge_checks.question: An estimate covers designing and building the product but omits testing cycles, user training, and deployment work. Which concept explains the gap?
options: Gold plating — the estimate includes more than was requested
Scope creep — requirements grew during the estimation process
Rolling wave planning — the omitted work will be estimated later
Project scope exceeds product scope — the work around the product was forgotten
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Project scope exceeds product scope — the work around the product was forgotten
knowledge_checks.explanation: Testing, training, and deployment are project scope: required work that isn't part of the product's feature set. Estimating only product work is the classic underestimation pattern. Nothing was added (so neither creep nor gold plating), and rolling wave defers detail deliberately — this omission wasn't deliberate.

knowledge_checks.question: A developer adds an unrequested 'bonus' export feature, confident the customer will love it. Why is this a scope failure rather than generosity?
options: Because the exam simply forbids any feature not written in the contract
Because customers never actually value features they didn't request
Because developers lack the skill to judge which features have value
Because it spends budget and adds unvalidated risk and maintenance no one approved
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Because it spends budget and adds unvalidated risk and maintenance no one approved
knowledge_checks.explanation: Gold plating consumes real resources, introduces defect and integration risk nobody assessed, and hands the customer a maintenance obligation they never chose — regardless of good intent. It isn't about rules for their own sake, customer ingratitude, or developer judgment; it's that value and cost decisions belong to the process.

knowledge_checks.question: A stakeholder frames a request as 'just a small tweak,' but technical review shows significant architectural rework. What should drive the decision about the change?
options: The stakeholder's framing, since they own the requirement being changed
The actual technical impact analysis, not the requester's characterization
The team's available slack — small tweaks can proceed when capacity exists
The precedent — if past tweaks were absorbed, this one should be too
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: The actual technical impact analysis, not the requester's characterization
knowledge_checks.explanation: Requesters often genuinely underestimate what looks simple from outside; decisions must rest on real analyzed impact. Framing isn't evidence, spare capacity doesn't make unassessed rework free, and 'we absorbed the last one' is how creep compounds rather than a justification.

title: Scope Statements, WBS, and the Scope Baseline

eco_mappings.task: Task 2: Develop and manage project scope

learning_objectives: List the components of a scope statement, including exclusions.
Explain WBS decomposition rules: deliverable-oriented, 100% rule, work packages.
Assemble the scope baseline and explain what it controls.

sections.title: The Scope Statement
sections.content:
The **project scope statement** documents what the project will deliver and the boundaries around it: the product scope description, major **deliverables**, **acceptance criteria**, **exclusions** (what the project will *not* do), assumptions, and constraints.

Exclusions are the underrated component: writing down what's out of scope prevents the later dispute where a stakeholder 'always assumed' something was included. Boundaries protect both sides.

sections.title: The WBS
sections.content:
The **work breakdown structure** decomposes the total scope into a deliverable-oriented hierarchy, bottoming out in **work packages** — units small enough to estimate, assign, and verify. Two rules govern it. The **100% rule**: the WBS captures *all* the work, and each level sums exactly to its parent — nothing extra, nothing missing. And **deliverable orientation**: elements are outcomes ('approved design,' 'migrated data'), not vague activities.

A work package like 'ongoing support' with no deliverable and no completion criterion violates the design — it can't be estimated or verified and needs further decomposition. The **WBS dictionary** carries each element's details: boundaries, acceptance criteria, owner.

sections.title: The Scope Baseline
sections.content: The **scope baseline** = approved scope statement + WBS + WBS dictionary. It's the reference against which scope performance is measured and deliverables are validated — and like every baseline, it changes only through change control. When a stakeholder claims a deliverable is incomplete, the baseline is what you check: is the expectation documented, or is it new scope arriving in the form of a complaint?

sections.title: Exam Perspective
sections.content: Scenario signals: disputes about 'what the price includes' trace to missing exclusions or vague statements; unestimatable work traces to under-decomposed packages; and 'the stakeholder expected X but it's nowhere in the documentation' is resolved against the baseline, with genuinely new needs routed through change control — not silently added, not dismissed.

exam_traps: Omitting exclusions and paying for it later in 'I assumed that was included' disputes.
Writing activity-shaped WBS entries ('ongoing support') that can't be estimated or verified.
Forgetting the scope baseline is three things — statement, WBS, and dictionary — not just the statement.

flashcards.definition: Approved scope statement + WBS + WBS dictionary — changed only through change control.
The WBS captures all the work and only the work — each level sums exactly to its parent.
The lowest WBS level — small enough to estimate, assign, and verify completion.

knowledge_checks.question: A stakeholder insists a capability is missing from a deliverable, but it appears nowhere in the scope statement, requirements, or WBS. What is the credited handling?
options: Add the capability immediately, since stakeholder satisfaction is the project goal
Validate against the documented baseline; route the new need through change control
Refuse to discuss the request at all, since it was never documented anywhere
Ask the stakeholder to escalate directly to the sponsor if they feel strongly
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Validate against the documented baseline; route the new need through change control
knowledge_checks.explanation: The baseline is the arbiter of what was agreed; an undocumented expectation, however sincere, is new scope if accepted — which means change control. Silent addition is creep, refusal to engage burns the relationship over a solvable gap, and premature escalation outsources a conversation the PM owns.

knowledge_checks.question: A WBS entry reads 'ongoing support' with no deliverable and no completion criterion. What does WBS design require?
options: Leaving it — support entries are a customary exception to the rules
Deleting it, since work that resists estimation doesn't belong in scope
Decomposing it into specific deliverables with completion criteria
Estimating it by analogy to support entries on previous projects
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Decomposing it into specific deliverables with completion criteria
knowledge_checks.explanation: Work packages must be deliverable-oriented and verifiable; a vague activity bucket can't be estimated, assigned, or checked complete. Deleting real work violates the 100% rule from the other direction, analogy-estimating a vague bucket compounds the vagueness, and there is no customary exception.

knowledge_checks.question: Why do exclusions belong in a scope statement when they describe work the project will NOT do?
options: They pad the document so the scope section looks appropriately thorough
They document boundary decisions before differing assumptions become disputes
They exist only for fixed-price contracts, where scope defines the price
They give the team grounds to reject any future stakeholder request
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: They document boundary decisions before differing assumptions become disputes
knowledge_checks.explanation: Explicit exclusions convert silent, conflicting assumptions into an agreed boundary while agreement is cheap. They matter in every contracting and delivery model, they're substance rather than padding, and they inform — not weaponize — future change discussions, which still go through change control on their merits.

title: Scope Validation and Control

eco_mappings.task: Task 2: Develop and manage project scope

learning_objectives: Sequence control quality (internal) before validate scope (external acceptance).
Run scope control: detect deviations, route changes, protect the baseline.
Handle acceptance disputes when documentation and expectations diverge.

sections.title: Verify Inside, Validate Outside
sections.content:
Two checks stand between 'built' and 'done,' in order. **Control quality** verifies deliverables internally against specifications — producing *verified deliverables*. **Validate scope** then obtains the customer or sponsor's formal acceptance — producing *accepted deliverables*. Internal sign-off, however rigorous, never substitutes for external acceptance: QA passing is the entry ticket to validation, not its replacement.

Validating incrementally at phase boundaries beats one grand acceptance at the end — disputes surface while they're small and fixable.

sections.title: Scope Control
sections.content:
**Scope control** keeps actual scope aligned with the baseline: monitoring what's being built against what was approved, catching informal additions (the stakeholder's hallway request a developer quietly started), and routing every real change through change control. When unplanned work is discovered mid-flight, the credited pattern is to stop it, assess it, and route it properly — plus reinforce the process so the bypass doesn't repeat.

Control also runs in the other direction: work quietly *dropped* (an informal agreement to skip two backlog items) needs the same formal treatment — documented decisions, appropriate approval.

sections.title: When Acceptance Goes Sideways
sections.content: The hard cases: a customer rejects a deliverable that meets every documented criterion (clarify the disconnect first — criteria gap, misunderstanding, or new expectation — before reworking or standing on paperwork); a deliverable passes criteria but visibly fails the underlying business need (raise it with stakeholders before treating validation as final — criteria are means, not ends); and internal pressure to 'mark it accepted' without the accepting party's actual sign-off (never — acceptance is the customer's act, not a status field).

sections.title: Exam Perspective
sections.content: The recurring discriminations: control quality vs. validate scope (who checks, against what), stopping-and-routing vs. absorbing informal work, and clarifying-first vs. reflexive rework in acceptance disputes. All three reward keeping the baseline meaningful and acceptance genuinely external.

exam_traps: Marking scope validated on the strength of internal QA sign-off alone.
Letting informally started work continue because stopping it feels wasteful.
Reworking immediately on verbal complaint when the deliverable meets documented criteria — clarify the disconnect first.

flashcards.definition: Internal verification against specifications — produces verified deliverables.
Formal external acceptance by customer or sponsor — produces accepted deliverables.
Keeping actual scope aligned to the baseline — catching both informal additions and informal drops.

knowledge_checks.question: What is the correct sequence between control quality and validate scope, and why?
options: Validate scope first — customer priorities should drive the internal testing order
Either order works equally, since both checks examine the very same deliverable
Control quality first — verify internally before seeking formal external acceptance
They are the same process merely performed by two different departments
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Control quality first — verify internally before seeking formal external acceptance
knowledge_checks.explanation: Deliverables are verified internally against spec, then presented for external acceptance — bringing unverified work to the customer wastes their acceptance act on things that may bounce. The checks differ in actor and question (spec compliance vs. formal acceptance), so neither ordering-indifference nor same-process is right.

knowledge_checks.question: Mid-sprint, the PM discovers a developer building a feature a stakeholder requested informally in a hallway. What is the credited response?
options: Let it finish — stopping now would waste the effort already invested
Retroactively add the feature to the baseline, since work has started
Publicly reprimand the developer as a lesson to the whole team
Halt the work, route the request through the change process, and reinforce the process
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Halt the work, route the request through the change process, and reinforce the process
knowledge_checks.explanation: Uncontrolled scope must stop and enter the process — sunk effort doesn't legitimize a bypass, and retroactive baselining rewards it. The developer likely acted in good faith on a stakeholder request, so the fix is process reinforcement, not public punishment.

knowledge_checks.question: A deliverable meets every documented acceptance criterion, but the PM can see it doesn't actually solve the business problem the project exists for. What should happen before validation is treated as final?
options: Nothing — meeting documented criteria is the definition of complete
Raise the gap with stakeholders, since criteria are means to the business end
Quietly rework the deliverable until it solves the problem, then validate
Assume the business problem changed and update the charter accordingly
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Raise the gap with stakeholders, since criteria are means to the business end
knowledge_checks.explanation: Acceptance criteria operationalize the business need — when they demonstrably fail to, the gap goes to stakeholders before validation closes. Standing on the paperwork ships a known miss; silent rework spends resources without authorization or confirmation the concern is right; and assuming the problem changed is a guess where a conversation is available.

title: Quality Planning, Assurance, and Control

eco_mappings.task: Task 7: Plan and optimize quality of products/deliverables

learning_objectives: Distinguish quality planning, assurance (process), and control (product).
Apply the core quality toolkit: Pareto analysis, root cause analysis, metrics validity.
Keep the quality management plan current as new defect types and evidence emerge.

sections.title: Plan, Assure, Control
sections.content:
Three distinct activities share the word 'quality.' **Quality planning** decides the standards, metrics, and verification approach for this project — written into the quality management plan. **Quality assurance** audits the *processes*: are we working the way the plan says, and are those processes actually capable of producing quality? **Quality control** inspects the *products*: do deliverables meet their criteria?

The shorthand: assurance is process-facing and preventive; control is product-facing and detective. An audit finding that a required review step is being skipped is assurance; a failed test is control.

sections.title: The Working Toolkit
sections.content:
**Pareto analysis** concentrates improvement effort where it pays: rank defect categories by frequency and attack the vital few causing most of the problems, rather than spreading effort evenly or chasing the most recent complaint. **Root cause analysis** (five whys, fishbone diagrams) turns recurring defects from an endless patching exercise into a solvable systemic problem.

Metrics need auditing too: a defect count improving while customer satisfaction stagnates suggests the metric no longer captures what customers experience as quality — investigate the metric's validity, don't just push it harder. And severity classifications must evolve: a new defect category that fits no existing severity level gets a proper classification and a plan update, not a force-fit.

sections.title: The Plan Is a Living Document
sections.content: Quality plans change on evidence, through deliberate decision: production issues from an edge-case category the coverage thresholds miss mean updating the plan and tests; a proposal to cut a testing type for budget means first assessing what that testing uniquely catches. What's never credited is silent drift — steps skipped under pressure without decision or documentation — or its mirror image, refusing to reconsider any quality step regardless of evidence.

sections.title: Exam Perspective
sections.content: Classification questions test assurance-vs-control (process audit vs. deliverable inspection). Scenario questions test the toolkit: recurring defects → root cause, not more inspection; many defect types → Pareto, not even spread; improving metric with flat outcomes → question the metric. And every quality-vs-pressure scenario rewards deliberate, documented adjustment over silent erosion.

exam_traps: Confusing assurance (process audits) with control (product inspection).
Spreading defect-fix effort evenly instead of Pareto-ranking the vital few causes.
Pushing a quality metric harder when it visibly no longer tracks customer-experienced quality.

flashcards.definition: Process-facing and preventive — auditing whether the processes are followed and capable.
Product-facing and detective — inspecting deliverables against their criteria.
Ranking causes by frequency to focus effort on the vital few driving most defects.

knowledge_checks.question: An audit finds the team has been skipping a documented review step, though all deliverables still pass their tests. Which quality activity produced this finding, and why does it matter despite passing tests?
options: Quality assurance — process audits, catching what passing tests don't cover
Quality control — it inspected the deliverables themselves and found them lacking
Quality planning — it discovered the quality plan itself was incorrectly written
Scope validation — the customer's formal acceptance surfaced the missing step
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Quality assurance — process audits, catching what passing tests don't cover
knowledge_checks.explanation: Process-facing findings are assurance's territory — and passing tests don't cover what the skipped review uniquely catches (design, maintainability, edge thinking). Control would flag failing products, planning wrote the standard rather than auditing it, and no customer act is described.

knowledge_checks.question: Nine defect categories are open; the team wants maximum improvement from limited effort. Which tool directs the investment?
options: A fishbone diagram for the defect reported most recently
Even distribution of effort across all nine categories for fairness
First-in-first-out processing of the defect backlog by report date
Pareto analysis — rank categories by frequency and attack the vital few
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Pareto analysis — rank categories by frequency and attack the vital few
knowledge_checks.explanation: Pareto's premise is that most defects flow from few causes — ranking by frequency concentrates limited effort where it moves the number most. Even spread and FIFO ignore impact entirely, and a fishbone on the newest report analyzes one cause without knowing whether it's a major one.

knowledge_checks.question: The team's tracked defect metric has improved steadily for two quarters, yet customer satisfaction with product quality is flat. What should the project manager investigate?
options: Nothing — satisfaction data always lags process metrics by several quarters
Whether the metric still captures what customers actually experience as quality
Whether the satisfaction survey vendor has a methodology problem
How to push the defect metric further, since improvement compounds eventually
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Whether the metric still captures what customers actually experience as quality
knowledge_checks.explanation: A proxy improving while the outcome it proxies stagnates is the signature of metric drift — perhaps usability or performance issues that defect counts never see. Assuming a fixed lag or blaming the survey dodges the question, and optimizing the same possibly-invalid metric harder invests in the disconnect.

title: Building the Project Schedule
learning_objectives: Sequence activities using the four dependency types plus leads and lags.
Classify dependencies as mandatory, discretionary, internal, or external.
Select estimation methods — analogous, parametric, bottom-up, three-point/PERT — by information available.

sections.title: From Scope to Network
sections.content:
Schedule building runs: decompose work packages into **activities**, sequence them into a **network diagram** with dependencies, estimate durations, then derive the schedule. Four logical relationships connect activities: **finish-to-start** (FS — predecessor finishes, successor starts; the overwhelmingly common case), **start-to-start** (SS), **finish-to-finish** (FF), and the rare **start-to-finish** (SF).

A **lag** inserts mandatory waiting into a dependency (pour concrete, then FS + 2-day lag for curing). A **lead** overlaps it (start successor 3 days before predecessor finishes). Lag is required delay — not float, which is schedule flexibility, and not something to 'compress away' without addressing the underlying constraint.

sections.title: Dependency Character
sections.content:
Each dependency is also classified by nature: **mandatory** (hard logic — physically or legally required), **discretionary** (soft logic — preferred practice, and the first place to look when compressing), **internal** (within the team's control), and **external** (a vendor, a regulator, another team — outside your control but still yours to track and plan around).

External dependencies deserve realistic durations: an approval agency's *actual historical* turnaround, not its best-case published figure, plus risk buffer for known variability. Removing an uncertain external activity from the schedule doesn't remove its uncertainty — it just hides a real risk.

sections.title: Estimating Durations
sections.content:
Method follows information: **analogous** (top-down from similar past work — fast, rough, fine for early passes), **parametric** (rate × quantity where a reliable rate exists), **bottom-up** (sum detailed components — accurate but slow, and watch for the coordination overhead the parts don't include), and **three-point**, using optimistic/most-likely/pessimistic values. The **PERT (beta) estimate** weights the most likely: E = (O + 4M + P) / 6. Triangular averages all three equally: E = (O + M + P) / 3.

Estimates embed assumptions about *who* does the work: durations built on a departed expert's pace need reassessment for the actual, current team — headcount equivalence is not capability equivalence.

sections.title: Exam Perspective
sections.content: Classification is directly tested: curing time between dependent activities is lag on an FS dependency, not float and not a separate activity. Best-case external durations, uniform planning detail regardless of certainty, and estimates that outlive their staffing assumptions are the recurring realism traps. For PERT arithmetic: O=4, M=6, P=14 → (4+24+14)/6 = 7.

exam_traps: Confusing lag (required waiting built into a dependency) with float (schedule flexibility).
Scheduling external approvals at best-case published turnaround despite known variability.
Keeping durations estimated for a departed expert after less experienced staff take over the work.

flashcards.definition: Mandatory waiting time inserted into a dependency — required delay, not flexibility.
E = (O + 4M + P) / 6 — three-point estimate weighting the most likely value.
Soft logic based on preferred practice — the first candidate when compressing a schedule.

knowledge_checks.question: Two sequential critical-path activities require a two-day curing wait between them. How is this represented in the schedule?
options: As lag on the finish-to-start dependency between the two activities
As float, since a gap exists between the two activities
As a separate, independent activity with its own resources assigned
It should be removed, since the schedule needs compression anyway
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: As lag on the finish-to-start dependency between the two activities
knowledge_checks.explanation: A mandatory waiting period inside a dependency is lag. Float is unused flexibility — this wait is required, not flexible. It consumes no resources, so it isn't an activity, and 'removing' it doesn't remove the physics of curing.

knowledge_checks.question: Using PERT (beta), what is the expected duration for O=4 days, M=6 days, P=14 days?
options: 8 days — the simple triangular average of the three points
6 days — the most likely value always dominates the estimate
10 days — the midpoint of the optimistic and pessimistic values
knowledge_checks.explanation: PERT beta weights the most likely value ×4: (4 + 24 + 14)/6 = 42/6 = 7. The triangular average (4+6+14)/3 = 8 is a different formula; taking M alone ignores the spread; and the O–P midpoint ignores M entirely.

knowledge_checks.question: A schedule uses a government agency's best-case published approval turnaround, though its actual processing times are known to vary widely and run longer. What should the project manager do?
options: Keep the published figure, since it is the officially citable number
Remove the approval from the critical path, since its duration is uncertain
Assume the agency will improve, since published targets create accountability
Re-plan using the agency's actual historical turnaround plus schedule risk buffer
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Re-plan using the agency's actual historical turnaround plus schedule risk buffer
knowledge_checks.explanation: Realistic scheduling uses observed behavior, not aspirational figures — plus buffer proportional to the known variability. Official citability doesn't make a number realistic, hiding the activity hides the risk without removing it, and hoping the agency improves is not a planning method.

title: Critical Path, Float, and Schedule Compression
learning_objectives: Identify the critical path and interpret total float and free float.
Choose between crashing and fast tracking, with each technique's cost.
Evaluate whether compression is even feasible before committing to it.

sections.title: Critical Path and Float
sections.content:
The **critical path** is the longest path through the schedule network — it determines the minimum project duration, and its activities have zero (or minimum) float. **Total float** is how long an activity can slip without delaying the project finish; **free float** is how long it can slip without delaying its immediate successor.

Delay on a critical activity delays the project, day for day. Delay on a non-critical activity consumes float first — and float is a shared buffer: once an activity eats the path's float, everything downstream on that path is critical too. A schedule built from best-case estimates with zero float anywhere is fragile by design: any single overrun cascades, and that fragility deserves challenge before baselining.

sections.title: Crashing vs. Fast Tracking
sections.content:
Two compression techniques, two different costs. **Crashing** adds resources to critical-path activities — it trades **cost** for time. **Fast tracking** overlaps activities normally done in sequence (turning discretionary FS dependencies into parallel work) — it trades **risk** (of rework) for time, at no direct added cost.

Selection follows the constraint: no budget available → fast tracking is the one to evaluate first; no tolerable rework risk → crashing. Both apply only to the critical path — compressing non-critical activities shortens nothing.

sections.title: Is Compression Even Possible?
sections.content:
Before choosing a technique, test feasibility: can these specific activities actually be shortened? Some work doesn't crash — a fixed-duration regulatory review gains nothing from extra staff, and adding people to deeply sequential or knowledge-heavy work can slow it down through coordination overhead (Brooks' law territory). Fast tracking needs dependencies that are genuinely discretionary — you cannot parallel-run what is physically sequential.

If neither technique works, the honest options are scope, quality trade-offs made visible, or the date — pretending otherwise just schedules a future failure.

sections.title: Exam Perspective
sections.content: Given 'no more budget,' fast tracking beats crashing. Given 'quality cannot be risked,' crashing beats fast tracking. Given 'add people to everything' — check whether the specific activities compress at all. And remember the diagnostic: an activity with float slipping is monitoring news; a critical activity slipping is a project-end-date event.

exam_traps: Crashing when the stated constraint is 'no additional budget' — that's fast tracking's case.
Compressing non-critical activities and expecting the end date to move.
Adding people to work that doesn't compress — fixed-duration or coordination-heavy activities.

flashcards.definition: The longest network path — sets minimum project duration; its activities have zero float.
Adding resources to critical activities — trades cost for time.
Overlapping normally sequential activities — trades rework risk for time.

knowledge_checks.question: A critical-path activity is two weeks late, the end date cannot move, and no additional budget exists. Which compression technique should be evaluated first, and why?
options: Fast tracking — overlapping sequential work re-sequences at no direct added cost
Crashing — additional resources reliably restore any schedule
Resource leveling — smoothing assignments recovers the two weeks
Scope reduction — quietly trimming deliverables protects the date
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Fast tracking — overlapping sequential work re-sequences at no direct added cost
knowledge_checks.explanation: With no budget for added resources, crashing is off the table; fast tracking compresses by overlapping where dependencies allow, paying in rework risk instead of money. Leveling typically extends rather than compresses schedules, and silent scope trimming bypasses change control.

knowledge_checks.question: The plan is to add staff to every activity on the critical path to recover a slip. What must be checked before spending the money?
options: Whether the specific activities can actually be shortened by added resources
Whether the non-critical paths should receive additional staff as well
Whether the added staff can also reduce the project's total float
Nothing — added resources shorten activities roughly proportionally
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Whether the specific activities can actually be shortened by added resources
knowledge_checks.explanation: Crashing only works on crashable activities: fixed-duration reviews don't accelerate, and coordination-heavy work can slow down with more people. Proportional speed-up is exactly the assumption to test, non-critical staffing shortens nothing, and float isn't something staff 'reduce.'

knowledge_checks.question: A schedule reaches baseline review with best-case durations and zero float on every path. What is the professional concern?
options: None — zero float indicates an efficiently optimized schedule
The schedule should be compressed further while morale is high
Any single overrun cascades through the whole downstream sequence with no buffer
Float should be added only to non-critical paths for appearance
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Any single overrun cascades through the whole downstream sequence with no buffer
knowledge_checks.explanation: Zero float everywhere plus optimistic durations means the first surprise delays the project — fragility, not efficiency. Baselining it institutionalizes that fragility; further compression deepens it; and float isn't cosmetic decoration to sprinkle on side paths.

title: Cost Estimating, Reserves, and Budget Development
learning_objectives: Match estimate precision to information available and stage (ROM through definitive).
Distinguish contingency reserve (known risks) from management reserve (unknown unknowns).
Build the cost baseline and reconcile it with funding constraints.

sections.title: Estimates Sharpen Over Time
sections.content:
Cost estimates carry declared precision: a **rough order of magnitude (ROM)** estimate early (commonly cited around −25%/+75%) narrows toward a **definitive** estimate (around ±5–10%) as information improves. Precision should match knowledge: near-term work with detailed information available deserves detailed estimating techniques; using ROM everywhere when better data exists produces needless imprecision, and false precision on far-future work wastes effort.

The same toolbox as durations applies — analogous, parametric, bottom-up, three-point — with bottom-up's known blind spot: summed component costs miss integration and coordination overhead unless explicitly added.

sections.title: Two Reserves, Two Purposes
sections.content:
**Contingency reserve** covers *identified* risks — sized by risk analysis, owned by the project manager, spent when a planned-for risk occurs. It's inside the **cost baseline**. **Management reserve** covers *unidentified* risks (unknown unknowns) — held above the baseline, released through management approval, not the PM's discretion.

Discipline follows the definitions: a known risk materializing draws contingency, not management reserve. Funding new scope from management reserve is a category error — scope changes go through change control and proper budgeting. And when risks close without occurring, their contingency returns to the pool with reserve adequacy reassessed — not quietly redirected into extra scope.

sections.title: Baseline Meets Funding Reality
sections.content:
The **cost baseline** is the time-phased approved budget — spending mapped over time, not one number. It must be reconciled with **funding constraints**: if funding arrives in fixed quarterly releases that don't match the planned spending curve, the plan adjusts to reality (re-phasing work, negotiating releases) rather than assuming money appears when needed.

Honest financial visibility also means tracking **committed** costs (signed purchase orders not yet invoiced) alongside actual spend — committed-but-uninvoiced obligations are real exposure that actual-only reporting understates.

sections.title: Exam Perspective
sections.content: Reserve classification is heavily tested: identified risk occurs → contingency; unforeseen event → management reserve (with approval); new scope → neither, use change control. Estimation scenarios reward precision-matched-to-knowledge and the bottom-up coordination-overhead correction. Funding scenarios reward reconciling the spending plan with the actual release schedule.

exam_traps: Drawing management reserve for an identified, planned-for risk — that's contingency's job.
Funding scope additions from reserves instead of change control and budgeting.
Reporting only actual spend while large committed purchase orders sit invisible.

flashcards.definition: Budget for identified risks — inside the baseline, PM-managed, spent when a planned-for risk occurs.
Budget for unknown unknowns — above the baseline, released by management approval.
The time-phased approved budget — the reference for cost performance measurement.

knowledge_checks.question: A risk that was identified and planned for months ago has just occurred. Which money covers it?
options: Management reserve, since actual risk events are by definition unforeseen
A formal change request asking the board to increase the project budget
Contingency reserve — it exists for exactly this: identified risks occurring
The performing department's operational budget, since the work is departmental
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Contingency reserve — it exists for exactly this: identified risks occurring
knowledge_checks.explanation: Identified, planned-for risks are contingency's exact purpose — the reserve was sized from this risk analysis. Management reserve covers what was never identified; a budget change request isn't needed for a planned-for draw; and pushing project risk costs onto operations misstates both budgets.

knowledge_checks.question: The sponsor asks to fund a new feature from management reserve, 'since the money is just sitting there.' What should the project manager explain?
options: Agree — management reserve is ultimately the sponsor's money to direct as they wish
Suggest drawing contingency reserve instead, since the new feature carries some risk
Refuse and end the conversation, since reserves are simply untouchable by anyone
Explain management reserve covers unforeseen risks; new scope needs change control
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Explain management reserve covers unforeseen risks; new scope needs change control
knowledge_checks.explanation: New scope is plannable work, not an unforeseen risk — funding it from any reserve strips protection the reserves exist to provide and bypasses scope governance. Swapping to contingency commits the same category error; flat refusal without the correct path leaves the sponsor's need unaddressed.

knowledge_checks.question: A bottom-up estimate sums forty small tasks' costs precisely, yet the work involves heavy cross-task coordination that appears nowhere in the sum. What correction applies?
options: Switch to a rough order of magnitude estimate, since bottom-up clearly failed
None — bottom-up estimates are the most accurate method by definition
Add an explicit allowance for the integration and coordination overhead
Assume coordination is negligible, since each individual task is small
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Add an explicit allowance for the integration and coordination overhead
knowledge_checks.explanation: Bottom-up's known blind spot is the work between the pieces — more small tasks means more coordination points, not fewer. The targeted fix is adding the missing overhead, not retreating to a cruder method or trusting the technique's reputation over its visible gap.

title: Earned Value: Measurement and Forecasting
learning_objectives: Compute and interpret PV, EV, AC, CV, SV, CPI, and SPI.
Select the right EAC formula for the variance's expected persistence.
Interpret TCPI and the classic mixed-signal patterns.

sections.title: The Three Base Numbers
sections.content:
Everything in earned value builds on three measurements at a status date. **PV (planned value)**: the budgeted cost of work *scheduled* to be done by now. **EV (earned value)**: the budgeted cost of work *actually* done by now. **AC (actual cost)**: what that work actually cost. **BAC (budget at completion)** is the total planned budget.

Worked example used throughout: BAC $100k, halfway point. PV = $50k (half the work was scheduled), EV = $40k (40% of the work is done), AC = $48k (spent so far).

sections.title: Variances and Indices
sections.content:
**CV = EV − AC** (negative = over cost): $40k − $48k = −$8k. **SV = EV − PV** (negative = behind schedule): $40k − $50k = −$10k. **CPI = EV / AC**: 40/48 ≈ 0.83 — each dollar spent earned 83 cents of planned work. **SPI = EV / PV**: 40/50 = 0.80 — progressing at 80% of planned pace.

Below 1.0 is unfavorable, above is favorable, for both indices. The classic misread: 'we've only spent $48k of the $50k planned — we're under budget!' Spending less than planned while doing even less work is *poor* performance (CPI < 1), not savings — being 'under budget and behind schedule' usually means exactly this.

sections.title: Forecasting: EAC, ETC, VAC, TCPI
sections.content:
**EAC (estimate at completion)** forecasts total cost — the formula choice depends on whether the variance will persist. Variance was a one-time anomaly, remaining work at plan: **EAC = AC + (BAC − EV)** = 48 + 60 = $108k. Current cost performance will continue: **EAC = BAC / CPI** = 100/0.83 ≈ $120k. Both cost and schedule pressure persist: EAC = AC + (BAC − EV)/(CPI × SPI).

**ETC = EAC − AC** (what's left to spend); **VAC = BAC − EAC** (negative = forecast overrun). **TCPI = (BAC − EV)/(BAC − AC)**: the efficiency required on remaining funds to hit BAC — here 60/52 ≈ 1.15, meaning every remaining dollar must earn $1.15 of work. TCPI far above achieved CPI is the honest signal that the original budget is no longer credible.

sections.title: Exam Perspective
sections.content: Formula selection outranks arithmetic: 'atypical, won't recur' → AC + (BAC − EV); 'trend continues' → BAC / CPI. Interpretation patterns: CPI 0.8 means over cost for work performed (not 'scope grew,' not 'behind schedule' — that's SPI's job); one good month doesn't make a trend (check multiple periods before re-forecasting); and healthy aggregates can hide offsetting work-package problems — drill down before declaring health.

exam_traps: Reading 'spent less than planned' as good news when even less work was done — that's CPI < 1.
Using BAC/CPI when the variance was explicitly a one-time anomaly (or vice versa).
Attributing schedule meaning to CPI or cost meaning to SPI — each index answers its own question.

flashcards.definition: EV ÷ AC — cost efficiency of work performed; below 1.0 means over cost.
EV ÷ PV — schedule efficiency; below 1.0 means behind planned pace.
BAC ÷ CPI — total cost forecast when current cost performance continues.
AC + (BAC − EV) — forecast when the variance won't recur and remaining work runs at plan.
(BAC − EV) ÷ (BAC − AC) — efficiency required on remaining funds to finish on budget.

knowledge_checks.question: A project reports CPI 0.8. What does this mean, precisely?
options: The project is currently running behind its planned schedule pace
The project's formally approved scope has grown by twenty percent
The project is under budget, having spent less money than planned
Each dollar spent earned only 80 cents of planned work — over cost
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Each dollar spent earned only 80 cents of planned work — over cost
knowledge_checks.explanation: CPI = EV/AC measures cost efficiency of the work actually done. Schedule pace is SPI's question, scope changes are change control's record, and 'under budget' misreads low spend that reflects even lower earning — the trap CPI exists to expose.

knowledge_checks.question: BAC = $200k, EV = $80k, AC = $100k. The overrun came from a one-time vendor failure not expected to recur. What is the appropriate EAC?
options: $250k — from BAC ÷ CPI, assuming the trend continues
$220k — from AC + (BAC − EV), remaining work at planned rate
$200k — the original BAC still stands as the forecast
EAC cannot be estimated until the project is complete
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: $220k — from AC + (BAC − EV), remaining work at planned rate
knowledge_checks.explanation: An atypical, non-recurring variance means remaining work is forecast at plan: 100 + (200 − 80) = $220k. BAC/CPI (= 200/0.8 = 250) assumes the poor efficiency persists — contradicting the premise; sticking with BAC ignores the $20k already lost; and waiting until completion is not forecasting.

knowledge_checks.question: A project is 'under budget' ($52k spent vs. $60k planned) and also behind schedule by a similar margin. What is the most likely correct reading?
options: Excellent cost management deserving recognition in the next status report
Evidence that the budget was simply set too high and should now be reduced
Less spent because less work is done — likely poor performance; check CPI
A finance recording error, since the two signals appear to contradict
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Less spent because less work is done — likely poor performance; check CPI
knowledge_checks.explanation: Underspend paired with schedule lag usually means low earned value: spending trails plan because progress does. Celebrating it mistakes the pattern; cutting the budget entrenches the misreading; and the signals don't contradict — they jointly point at exactly this diagnosis.

title: Financial Selection Metrics: ROI, NPV, IRR, and Payback
learning_objectives: Interpret NPV, IRR, ROI, payback period, and benefit-cost ratio as selection signals.
Apply the comparison rules: higher NPV wins; NPV > 0 creates value.
Distinguish sunk cost and opportunity cost and use each correctly in decisions.

sections.title: The Time Value Family
sections.content:
Money later is worth less than money now — **present value (PV)** discounts future cash to today. **NPV (net present value)** sums all discounted inflows minus outflows: **NPV > 0 means the investment creates value** after the discount rate is paid; between alternatives, **higher NPV wins**. NPV already accounts for timing — no extra adjustment needed for one option paying off later.

**IRR (internal rate of return)** is the discount rate at which NPV hits zero — effectively the investment's earning rate; higher is better, and it must clear the organization's hurdle rate to be worth doing.

sections.title: Simpler Signals
sections.content:
**ROI** = (benefit − cost) / cost, a percentage return ignoring timing. **Payback period** = time until cumulative inflows repay the investment — a liquidity/risk signal, blind to everything after payback and to the time value of money. **Benefit-cost ratio (BCR)** > 1 means benefits exceed costs.

Because the simple metrics ignore timing (and payback ignores post-payback benefits entirely), organizations comparing serious alternatives lean on NPV; payback matters most when capital is tight or early recovery reduces risk exposure.

sections.title: Sunk and Opportunity Cost
sections.content:
**Sunk cost** is money already spent — unrecoverable, and *irrelevant* to any forward-looking decision. 'We've invested too much to stop' is the sunk cost fallacy; the only valid comparison is remaining cost versus remaining value. **Opportunity cost** is the value of the best alternative foregone by choosing this option — real, forward-looking, and frequently what a selection question is actually asking about.

These two are exam favorites precisely because human intuition gets them backwards: we cling to sunk costs and ignore opportunity costs.

sections.title: Exam Perspective
sections.content: Selection questions: given competing projects, pick the higher NPV unless the question explicitly weights liquidity (payback) or hurdle rates (IRR). Interpretation questions: NPV −$50k means the project destroys value at the discount rate, regardless of a positive raw ROI. And any 'we've already spent $X' argument at a gate review is sunk cost bait — the credited reasoning uses current data and remaining value only.

exam_traps: Letting money already spent influence a continue/stop decision — sunk cost is decision-irrelevant.
Choosing by shortest payback when the question gives NPV — payback ignores time value and later benefits.
Reading NPV > 0 as merely 'profitable' — it means value creation after the discount rate is already paid.

flashcards.definition: Discounted inflows minus outflows; positive = creates value; higher wins between alternatives.
The discount rate at which NPV equals zero — the investment's effective earning rate.
Money already spent — unrecoverable and irrelevant to forward-looking decisions.
The value of the best alternative foregone by committing resources here.

knowledge_checks.question: Project A has NPV $120k; Project B has NPV $95k but pays back sooner and 'feels safer.' Using standard selection logic with no stated liquidity constraint, which is chosen?
options: Project A — higher NPV wins when no other constraint is specified
Project B — earlier payback always dominates other financial signals
Either — NPV differences under $50k are within estimation noise
Neither — IRR must be computed before any selection is possible
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Project A — higher NPV wins when no other constraint is specified
knowledge_checks.explanation: NPV is the default comparator because it already prices timing via the discount rate — payback only takes precedence when the question explicitly raises liquidity or capital constraints. No 'noise threshold' rule exists, and IRR is an alternative lens, not a prerequisite.

knowledge_checks.question: At a gate review, a struggling project's sponsor argues: 'We've already spent $3M — stopping now wastes it.' What is the financially sound framing?
options: The $3M justifies continuing until at least partial value is recovered
The $3M is sunk either way; compare remaining cost against remaining value
The $3M should be amortized into the continuation budget for fairness
The decision should defer to whoever authorized the original $3M
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: The $3M is sunk either way; compare remaining cost against remaining value
knowledge_checks.explanation: Sunk cost is unrecoverable under every future choice, so it cannot differentiate the options — only the forward comparison matters. 'Recovering' sunk cost by spending more is the fallacy itself, amortizing it into future budgets double-counts it, and deference to the original authorizer substitutes politics for analysis.

knowledge_checks.question: A proposal shows ROI of +18% but NPV of −$40k at the organization's discount rate. How can both be true, and what does it mean?
options: They cannot both be true — one of the two calculations must contain an error
The NPV should be recomputed without discounting so it matches the ROI figure
ROI ignores timing; at the required rate the returns arrive too late to create value
The proposal creates value, since a positive ROI overrides any negative NPV
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: ROI ignores timing; at the required rate the returns arrive too late to create value
knowledge_checks.explanation: ROI compares raw totals with no time dimension; NPV prices when the money moves. Returns that look positive undiscounted can fall below the hurdle once timing is priced — meaning the investment destroys value at the required rate. Undiscounting NPV defeats its purpose, and ROI doesn't override it.

title: Identifying and Analyzing Stakeholders
learning_objectives: Identify stakeholders by actual influence and interest, not job titles.
Apply analysis models: power/interest grid, salience model, engagement assessment matrix.
Keep stakeholder analysis current as the project and its context change.

sections.title: Identification: Influence, Not Titles
sections.content:
A **stakeholder** is anyone who affects, or is affected by, the project. Identification fails most often by proxy: relying on the org chart and missing real influence — the regulatory liaison with release-blocking authority, the executive assistant who controls what reaches a decision-maker, the end users represented only by their managers. Managers speaking 'for' users is a convenient assumption that reliably misses what users would actually say.

Identification is also never finished: new markets, new phases, and reorganizations introduce stakeholders the original register never anticipated.

sections.title: Analysis Models
sections.content:
Analysis turns the list into strategy. The **power/interest grid** sorts stakeholders into four postures: high power/high interest → *manage closely*; high power/low interest → *keep satisfied*; low power/high interest → *keep informed*; low power/low interest → *monitor*. The **salience model** refines prioritization with three attributes — **power** (ability to impose will), **legitimacy** (appropriateness of involvement), and **urgency** (need for immediate attention) — stakeholders holding all three demand priority.
