The **stakeholder engagement assessment matrix** compares each stakeholder's *current* engagement level (unaware, resistant, neutral, supportive, leading) against the *desired* level — the gaps are the engagement plan's to-do list.

sections.title: Analysis Decays
sections.content:
Stakeholder positions move: a resistant stakeholder becomes an advocate (keep investing — and consider their influence on other resisters); an engaged one goes quiet (investigate, don't assume satisfaction); committee membership turns over (revisit what 'approved' meant to people no longer in the room). Direction of influence matters too — upward (sponsors, executives), downward (team), outward (vendors, regulators, customers), sideward (peer PMs competing for the same resources).

Re-analysis triggers: phase transitions, scope changes, org changes, and any surprising stakeholder behavior.

sections.title: Exam Perspective
sections.content: Credited options identify by real influence (adding the mid-project regulatory liaison to the register and engaging them now, not at release), analyze before engaging (a fresh analysis for genuinely new stakeholder populations), and treat informal influence as real (the assistant filtering the executive's inputs). Distractors defer to titles, freeze the register at initiation, or route around inconvenient stakeholders.

exam_traps: Identifying stakeholders from the org chart while real influence sits elsewhere.
Letting managers 'represent' end users instead of engaging users directly.
Treating the initiation-time register as final while the stakeholder landscape shifts.

flashcards.definition: Manage closely / keep satisfied / keep informed / monitor — posture by power and interest.
Prioritizes stakeholders by power, legitimacy, and urgency — all three demands priority.
Current vs. desired engagement level per stakeholder — the gaps drive the plan.

knowledge_checks.question: A security architect with de facto authority to block release has never been included in planning, because their role has low visibility on the org chart. What does sound stakeholder practice require?
options: Include them in relevant planning now — actual influence over outcomes defines a stakeholder
Continue as-is, since the charter's stakeholder list controls who participates
Wait until the release approval stage, when their role becomes formally active
Ask a more senior architect to informally represent their concerns instead
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Include them in relevant planning now — actual influence over outcomes defines a stakeholder
knowledge_checks.explanation: Stakeholder engagement follows real influence, not chart visibility — discovering blocking concerns at release time maximizes rework. The charter list isn't a permission boundary, deferral guarantees late surprises, and proxy representation loses exactly the specific concerns that can block release.

knowledge_checks.question: In the power/interest grid, what is the standard posture for a high-power, LOW-interest stakeholder?
options: Manage closely, with detailed hands-on involvement in every project decision
Monitor only, with no proactive communication effort directed their way
Keep informed through frequent, detailed working-level status updates
Keep satisfied — enough attention to stay comfortable, without detail floods
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Keep satisfied — enough attention to stay comfortable, without detail floods
knowledge_checks.explanation: High power demands care, low interest demands economy: keep them satisfied without burying them in detail they don't want — over-consulting powerful-but-uninterested stakeholders breeds disengagement. Manage-closely is for high/high, monitoring is for low/low, and detailed working updates fit high-interest profiles.

knowledge_checks.question: The project expands into two new geographic markets with entirely new local stakeholder populations. What does the engagement approach require?
options: Extending the existing engagement plan unchanged, since it was formally approved
Fresh identification and analysis for the new markets — local context changes influence and interest
Relying on original-market stakeholders to represent the new markets' interests
Deferring new-market engagement until the expansion work is further along
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Fresh identification and analysis for the new markets — local context changes influence and interest
knowledge_checks.explanation: New populations mean new influence structures, interests, and local considerations the original analysis had no way to capture. Extending the old plan assumes context doesn't matter, borrowed representation substitutes the wrong voices, and deferral repeats the discover-stakeholders-late failure.

title: Engagement and Communications Planning
learning_objectives: Choose communication methods — interactive, push, pull — by purpose.
Apply the channels formula and what it implies about team size.
Keep the communication plan effective: tailored, current, and structurally sound.

sections.title: Methods and Tailoring
sections.content:
Three method families: **interactive** (conversation — meetings, calls; best for resolving, deciding, de-escalating), **push** (sent to recipients — email, reports; efficient, but delivery ≠ understanding), and **pull** (recipients retrieve — dashboards, repositories; right for reference material and large audiences).

Effective plans tailor by audience: format to preference (executives skim — lead with what matters; engineers need detail), channel to urgency (critical alerts don't ride routine email), and language to reality (a stakeholder group that can't comfortably read the reports isn't being communicated with). When engagement drops, adapt the plan — repetition of a failing format is not persistence, it's noise.

sections.title: Channels Grow Quadratically
sections.content:
Communication complexity scales with the square of team size: **channels = n(n−1)/2**. Five people → 10 channels; ten people → 45; doubling the team more than quadruples the pathways where misunderstanding can live.

The implication isn't 'keep teams tiny' — it's that larger groups need deliberate structure: defined channels, documented decisions, and information radiators, because informal osmosis stops scaling. Symptoms of osmosis failure: co-located subgroups deciding in hallways while remote members learn after the fact, and important decisions living in nobody-knows-which of five tools.

sections.title: Structural Soundness
sections.content:
Communication plans need maintenance and failure-mode design: the recipient list must track the *current* stakeholder register, not initiation's; urgent issues need a defined escalation path that doesn't depend on one person's availability (single points of contact fail on weekends); and decisions need a documented home every member can access, regardless of location or time zone.

Measurement closes the loop: declining open rates, missed messages, and 'I never saw that' incidents are data about the plan, and the credited response is diagnosis and adaptation, not blame or volume.

sections.title: Exam Perspective
sections.content: Match method to purpose (de-escalation → interactive, never email); fix structure, not people (missed messages across five tools → consolidate channels; hallway decisions → documented decision channel); and adapt to evidence (skimmed reports → restructure for skimmability; disengaged group → investigate and tailor). The wrong answers add volume, add tools, or add blame.

exam_traps: Sending more email into a channel that's demonstrably failing to reach its audience.
Leaving urgent escalation dependent on one person's availability.
Forgetting channel math — team growth quadratically multiplies communication pathways.

flashcards.definition: n(n−1)/2 — pathways grow with the square of team size.
Two-way conversation — the method for resolving, deciding, and de-escalating.
Recipients retrieve at need — dashboards and repositories for reference material.

knowledge_checks.question: A project team grows from 5 to 10 people. How do communication channels change?
options: They double from 10 to 20, tracking the headcount increase linearly
They rise from 10 to 45 — more than quadrupling as headcount doubles
They rise from 5 to 10, adding exactly one channel for each new person
They are unaffected — channel count depends on tools rather than people
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: They rise from 10 to 45 — more than quadrupling as headcount doubles
knowledge_checks.explanation: n(n−1)/2: 5×4/2 = 10 and 10×9/2 = 45. The quadratic growth is the point — informal communication that worked at five people structurally cannot survive at ten without deliberate channels and documentation.

knowledge_checks.question: Critical updates keep getting buried in stakeholders' overflowing inboxes. Which response fixes the actual problem?
options: Send each critical update several times to improve the odds that one lands
Stop written updates entirely and rely on recall from the live meetings
Evaluate a more visible channel or format — the current one isn't cutting through
Keep the current approach, since email is the organization's standard tool
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Evaluate a more visible channel or format — the current one isn't cutting through
knowledge_checks.explanation: A channel that demonstrably fails its critical messages needs rethinking — distinct alerts, a dashboard, or format changes. Repetition amplifies the very flood burying the messages, dropping written records loses the durable reference, and 'it's the standard' is how failing channels persist.

knowledge_checks.question: A critical production issue arose over a weekend; the communication plan routes all urgent escalation through the project manager, who was unreachable. What is the structural fix?
options: The project manager commits to unlimited availability going forward
The team waits for the project manager in any future urgent situation
Urgent issues get downgraded to routine handling when the PM is away
A defined backup escalation contact and process enters the communication plan
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: A defined backup escalation contact and process enters the communication plan
knowledge_checks.explanation: One person as the sole urgent path is a single point of failure — the durable fix is structural: a named backup and process. Unlimited personal availability is neither sustainable nor reliable, and waiting or downgrading during real emergencies just re-runs this incident.

title: Resource Planning and Acquisition
learning_objectives: Plan both human and physical resources, including lead times and calendars.
Resolve overallocation through leveling, smoothing, and cross-project negotiation.
Keep resource assumptions honest: capability differences, ramp-up, and known absences.

sections.title: More Than Staffing
sections.content:
Resource planning covers people *and* physical resources — equipment, facilities, materials. Physical resources carry their own planning obligations: **procurement lead times** built into the schedule (discovering a long-lead component the week it's needed is a planning failure, not bad luck), shared assets coordinated across teams before conflicts hit, and **resource calendars** capturing real availability — including mandated leave, holidays, and part-time allocations.

Known future constraints (an announced facility renovation, a required leave block) are planning inputs the moment they're known — not surprises to react to when they arrive.

sections.title: Overallocation and Its Cures
sections.content:
A resource committed beyond capacity — 150% across three projects, or 100% here plus 50% there — is a planning conflict that never resolves itself; someone silently under-delivers. Within a project, **resource leveling** delays or resequences work to fit real capacity (often extending the schedule — an honest extension beats a fictional date); **resource smoothing** adjusts within existing float, protecting the end date but fixing less. Across projects, the conflict exceeds any one PM's authority: it goes to the resource/portfolio owner or the sponsors for a joint priority call.

The anti-patterns: splitting a person evenly regardless of priorities, assigning by who asked first, and asking the individual to just absorb both commitments.

sections.title: Honest Capacity Assumptions
sections.content: Plans quietly embed capability assumptions worth auditing: headcount is not capacity (junior and senior output differ; two juniors don't equal the departed expert whose pace set the estimates); ramp-up time is real and varies with background; and matching *skills* to tasks beats assigning whoever's free — availability-based assignment leaves quality and efficiency on the table. When the organization's staffing model changes (flexible reassignment every few months), the plan changes too: onboarding time and knowledge-transfer checkpoints become recurring line items, not surprises.

sections.title: Exam Perspective
sections.content: Signals: a long-lead item discovered late → the lesson is identifying lead times during planning; a cross-project resource conflict → escalate for joint prioritization, don't split arbitrarily; a departed expert's estimates → reassess against actual staff; a mandated absence not in the schedule → plan around it now (cross-train, redistribute, adjust), never pressure the person to skip entitled leave.

exam_traps: Treating headcount as capacity while capability and ramp-up differences are known.
Splitting an overallocated person evenly between projects instead of getting priorities decided.
Reacting to known future constraints (leave, renovations, lead times) only when they arrive.

flashcards.definition: Resequencing or delaying work to fit real resource capacity — may extend the schedule.
Adjusting within existing float — protects the end date but resolves less.
Commitment beyond capacity — a planning conflict that someone silently absorbs unless resolved.

knowledge_checks.question: Two projects need the same senior DBA at full capacity for the same three weeks, and neither schedule has slack. What is the credited first move?
options: Escalate to the portfolio owner or both sponsors for a joint priority decision
Split the DBA's time evenly between projects so neither one feels disadvantaged
Assign the DBA to whichever of the two projects submitted its request first
Ask the DBA to work extended hours and personally cover both commitments
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Escalate to the portfolio owner or both sponsors for a joint priority decision
knowledge_checks.explanation: A genuine cross-project conflict with no slack exceeds either PM's authority — it needs a priority call from whoever sees both efforts. Even splits under-resource both projects, first-come ignores business priority, and 'just work more' converts a planning failure into personal burnout.

knowledge_checks.question: A construction milestone depends on materials with a long procurement lead time that never made it into the schedule, discovered the week the materials are needed. What is the planning lesson?
options: Order all materials at project start regardless of when they're needed
Identify long-lead resource needs during planning and build them into the schedule
Add a standard two-week buffer to every milestone as general protection
Treat material delays as force majeure outside project accountability
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Identify long-lead resource needs during planning and build them into the schedule
knowledge_checks.explanation: Physical-resource lead times are plannable, knowable inputs — this failure was in planning, not luck. Ordering everything immediately ties up capital and storage; generic buffers are imprecise insurance against a specific, identifiable need; and 'force majeure' mislabels a foreseeable omission.

knowledge_checks.question: Duration estimates were built around a senior expert who has left; two less experienced people now split that work. The person-hours available are similar. What should happen to the estimates?
options: Nothing — similar total person-hours means similar total capacity
Add a blanket project-wide buffer without touching the specific estimates
Reassess the affected activities against the actual, current team's pace and capability
Assume the two replacements will jointly outpace the original expert
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Reassess the affected activities against the actual, current team's pace and capability
knowledge_checks.explanation: Estimates encode who does the work — expertise and pace don't transfer by headcount arithmetic. Hour-equivalence is the exact fallacy, a generic buffer smears the correction across unaffected work, and assuming the replacements outpace the expert is optimism, not analysis.

title: Team Leadership and Development
learning_objectives: Apply the Tuckman stages and the leadership behavior each stage needs.
Build team foundations: working agreements, ownership clarity, development opportunity.
Lead distributed teams deliberately — equal access, designed connection.

sections.title: Tuckman's Stages, Leader's Dial
sections.content:
Teams develop through **forming** (polite, guarded, orienting — needs clear direction and deliberate trust-building), **storming** (friction over approaches and roles — needs facilitation and conflict navigation, not suppression), **norming** (working agreements settle — needs reinforcement), **performing** (self-directed, high output — needs delegation and light touch), and **adjourning** (wrap-up — needs closure and recognition).

The leadership dial moves with the stage: directive early, facilitative through friction, delegating at maturity. The two classic errors are stage-blind: staying directive with a performing team (reads as distrust, throttles the performance) and going hands-off with a forming one (abandonment, not empowerment). Regression happens too — a re-formed or reorganized team revisits earlier stages.

sections.title: Foundations That Prevent Problems
sections.content:
New teams skip foundations at their own cost: **working agreements** (roles, communication norms, definition of done) turn miscommunication and duplicated work from chronic into rare; **ownership clarity** for cross-cutting tasks prevents the everyone's-job-is-no-one's-job pattern behind many missed deadlines; and **development** — matching stretch opportunities to skill and potential, not just to who volunteers loudest — keeps quieter members growing instead of stagnating in routine work.

Building capability internally (upskilling with lead time) frequently beats renting it, when timeline allows and the organization wants to retain the capability.

sections.title: Distributed Teams Need Design
sections.content: Remote and hybrid teams lose the accidental infrastructure of co-location — hallway decisions, osmotic context, spontaneous rapport — and it must be rebuilt deliberately: decisions documented where every member can reach them regardless of location; meeting times that don't structurally disadvantage the same time zone every week (rotate, record, or split); and informal connection created through optional structures, since it no longer happens by proximity. 'Remote members always find out last' is a design failure, not a geography fact.

sections.title: Exam Perspective
sections.content: Stage questions reward matching behavior to development stage — clear direction for forming, facilitation for storming, delegation for performing. Team-problem questions reward structural fixes (agreements, ownership, channels) over exhortation or blame. Distributed-team questions reward equal-access design over 'ask the office folks to relay things.'

exam_traps: Applying performing-stage delegation to a forming team, or forming-stage direction to a performing one.
Fixing coordination problems with more meetings instead of working agreements and ownership.
Letting informal, location-based information flow decide who on a distributed team stays informed.

flashcards.definition: Polite, guarded orientation — the stage needing clear direction and deliberate trust-building.
Friction over roles and approaches — needs facilitation, not suppression; a normal stage, not a failure.
Explicit roles, norms, and definition of done — the structural cure for chronic miscommunication.

knowledge_checks.question: A newly formed team is polite but won't challenge each other's ideas, producing shallow design discussions. Which stage is this, and what does the leader do?
options: Performing — delegate more authority so the team can self-organize
Storming — mediate the underlying disagreements between members directly
Norming — step back and let the established norms operate on their own
Forming — give clear direction and deliberately build the trust that enables challenge
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Forming — give clear direction and deliberately build the trust that enables challenge
knowledge_checks.explanation: Guarded politeness with no productive disagreement is forming: members are still orienting and don't yet feel safe to push. The leader supplies structure and builds psychological safety so the team can storm productively. The other options prescribe behaviors for stages this team hasn't reached.

knowledge_checks.question: A team needs a machine-learning skill nobody has; work starts in three months, budget is tight, and the organization wants to keep the capability afterward. What is the strongest option?
options: Delay the project until a qualified specialist can be recruited externally
Upskill an interested internal team member through training and mentoring
Hire an external contractor with the skill for the project's duration
Outsource the machine-learning work package to a specialist vendor
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Upskill an interested internal team member through training and mentoring
knowledge_checks.explanation: Three months of lead time, a constrained budget, and a retention goal all point the same direction: build the capability internally. Contractors and vendors cost more and take the knowledge with them, and delaying is unnecessary given the runway available.

knowledge_checks.question: Remote team members repeatedly learn about decisions after the fact, because discussion happens informally among co-located colleagues. What is the credited fix?
options: Ask the co-located members to remember to relay decisions informally each time
Require the whole team to relocate to a single office for the project's duration
Establish norms and tools giving every member equal access to decisions
Document decisions only once final, to avoid slowing down the office group
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Establish norms and tools giving every member equal access to decisions
knowledge_checks.explanation: Location-based information inequality is a structural problem — the fix is structural: documented decisions in shared channels, norms that move decisions out of hallways. Informal relay preserves the inequity with extra steps, relocation is disproportionate, and after-the-fact documentation is precisely the current failure.

title: Conflict Resolution and Negotiation
learning_objectives: Apply the five conflict approaches and know when each fits.
Resolve conflict with objective criteria, close to the source, at the root.
Negotiate on interests rather than positions.

sections.title: Five Approaches, One Default
sections.content:
The five classic responses: **collaborate/problem-solve** (dig into the underlying issue for a durable win-win — the default when time and stakes allow), **compromise** (each side yields; moderately satisfying, moderately durable), **smooth/accommodate** (emphasize agreement, cede the point — for preserving relationships on low-stakes issues), **force/direct** (impose — for emergencies and non-negotiables like safety, at relationship cost), and **withdraw/avoid** (postpone or exit — occasionally right for cooling off, never a resolution).

Collaboration earns its default status because it alone resolves the *cause*; everything else manages the symptom at varying cost.

sections.title: Resolution Discipline
sections.content:
Three principles do most of the work. **Objective criteria**: steer technical disputes to evaluation against requirements, data, and agreed standards — defensible, repeatable, and it depersonalizes the fight. **Close to the source**: those nearest the disagreement resolve it, with facilitation; escalation comes after genuine attempts fail or when authority is truly exceeded. **Root cause**: recurring conflicts have a generator — a values difference, a structural ambiguity, suppressed dissent — and settling instances while the generator persists guarantees sequels.

Watch the aftermath too: an agreement reached because someone felt outnumbered isn't resolution — check in, and reopen if it isn't durable. And when conflict turns personal (public jabs, disengagement), the interpersonal harm needs its own direct attention, separate from the substantive issue.

sections.title: Interests, Not Positions
sections.content:
A **position** is what someone demands; an **interest** is why they demand it. Positions collide ('I need the resource in March' vs. 'I need them in March too'); interests often don't (one needs a report generated, the other needs migration expertise — divisible). Negotiating on interests — asking *why* until the real needs surface, then designing options that satisfy both — turns zero-sum standoffs into solvable design problems.

With external parties (vendors, contractors), the same logic applies, with influence substituting for authority: clarify the shared interest (a working deliverable, a continuing relationship), and use the contract as a framework rather than a weapon.

sections.title: Exam Perspective
sections.content: Defaults: collaborate when feasible; objective criteria for technical disputes; direct engagement before escalation; root cause for anything recurring. Recognize the misfits — forcing a resolvable disagreement, avoiding an active blocker, splitting the difference on a safety issue — and the aftermath traps: treating suppressed agreement as consensus, and leaving personal damage unaddressed because the technical question got settled.

exam_traps: Escalating a conflict before those closest to it have genuinely attempted resolution.
Settling recurring conflict instances while the underlying generator keeps producing them.
Reading an outnumbered stakeholder's silence as genuine agreement.

flashcards.definition: Dig into the underlying issue for a durable resolution — the default when time and stakes allow.
Requirements, data, and agreed standards as the referee for technical disputes.
Positions are demands; interests are the reasons — negotiate the reasons.

knowledge_checks.question: Two senior developers' technical disagreement has stalled sprint work; both have valid arguments. What is the credited first move?
options: Escalate to the sponsor so an authoritative decision unblocks the work
Facilitate an evaluation of both options against documented requirements and criteria
Reassign one developer so the disagreement physically cannot continue
Decide personally as project manager, since the schedule is slipping
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Facilitate an evaluation of both options against documented requirements and criteria
knowledge_checks.explanation: Technical disputes between capable people resolve best against objective criteria, close to the source — it settles this dispute and models how to settle the next one. Escalation and PM fiat move the decision away from the expertise, and reassignment avoids rather than resolves.

knowledge_checks.question: Two stakeholders each cite a different informal promise made by different project staff. How should the priority conflict be resolved?
options: Honor whichever informal promise was chronologically made first
Let the two stakeholders negotiate it between themselves without facilitation
Escalate immediately to the sponsor before attempting any resolution
Facilitate resolution against the project's documented priority criteria
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Facilitate resolution against the project's documented priority criteria
knowledge_checks.explanation: Documented criteria give a defensible, repeatable basis that also fixes the root cause — priorities being set informally. First-promise-wins rewards process bypass, unfacilitated negotiation between the aggrieved rarely lands well, and immediate escalation skips the resolution the PM can facilitate.

knowledge_checks.question: In interest-based negotiation, what distinguishes an interest from a position?
options: A position is what someone demands; an interest is why they need it
An interest is the opening offer; a position is the final settlement
Positions are held by buyers while interests belong to sellers
The two terms describe the same thing at different meeting stages
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: A position is what someone demands; an interest is why they need it
knowledge_checks.explanation: The demand (position) is one proposed way of meeting the underlying need (interest) — and different interests can often both be satisfied where identical positions cannot. The other options invent distinctions — offer sequence, buyer/seller roles, meeting stages — that aren't the concept.

title: Risk Planning and Identification
learning_objectives: Structure risk identification as an inclusive, recurring, multi-category activity.
Record risks properly: register entries with owners and planned responses.
Distinguish threats from opportunities and treat both as risks.

sections.title: Risk Is Uncertainty That Matters
sections.content:
A **risk** is an uncertain event or condition that, if it occurs, affects at least one project objective — and the effect can be negative (**threat**) or positive (**opportunity**). Real risk management works both sides: identifying only threats leaves value on the table, and identifying only technical risks leaves whole categories — organizational, financial, external, compliance — unexamined.

A **risk breakdown structure (RBS)** of categories is the standard antidote to one-sided identification: it prompts the questions a technically-minded room won't ask itself.

sections.title: Identification Is Inclusive and Recurring
sections.content:
Who identifies matters: a register written by the project manager alone reliably misses risks the team knew about but was never asked for. Identification draws on the whole team, stakeholders, experts, historical information — through brainstorming, checklists, assumption analysis, and lessons from past projects.

And it repeats: identification at initiation is a snapshot, not an inventory. New phases, major changes, and shifted external conditions all trigger reassessment. A register untouched since kickoff while the project entered new territory isn't stability — it's abandonment.

sections.title: Recording That Actually Manages
sections.content:
The **risk register** entry that manages (rather than merely lists) carries: description, category, assessed probability and impact, a **risk owner** (accountable for watching and responding — and reassigned promptly when the owner leaves), and a **planned response**. A low-probability, high-impact threat is a textbook case for documented response plus owner — passive acceptance because 'it probably won't happen' ignores the severity half of the assessment.

Risk plans decay like any plan: response cost estimates age (inflation, market shifts), and responses built around a specific person's expertise break when that person leaves. Both need refreshing for the register to stay executable.

sections.title: Exam Perspective
sections.content: Signals: a register written solo → make identification inclusive, don't just patch the found gaps; all-technical risks → target the missing categories with the right participants; register untouched across major changes → facilitate reassessment; risk owner departed → reassign now, not when the risk fires. And 'we identified it, so we're done' is never credited — identification without owner and response is a list, not management.

exam_traps: Building the register from one perspective (the PM's, or one discipline's) and missing whole categories.
Accepting a low-probability risk without weighing its severe impact.
Leaving a departed owner's risks unowned — unowned risks are unmonitored risks.

flashcards.definition: An uncertain event or condition that would affect objectives — negatively (threat) or positively (opportunity).
The person accountable for monitoring a risk and executing its response.
A category hierarchy that prompts identification beyond the room's default perspective.

knowledge_checks.question: A risk workshop produced a register that is almost entirely technical risks, with organizational, financial, and external categories nearly empty. What is the credited response?
options: Run targeted identification for the missing categories, involving different perspectives
Accept the register, since the attending engineers are the subject matter experts
Assume non-technical risks are minor for this project until one materializes
Close identification and revisit only if a non-technical risk actually occurs
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Run targeted identification for the missing categories, involving different perspectives
knowledge_checks.explanation: Category skew signals the room's composition, not the project's risk profile — different categories need different eyes. Engineer expertise doesn't cover finance or market risks, assuming the gaps are minor is unverified, and waiting for materialization forfeits the point of proactive identification.

knowledge_checks.question: The team identifies a low-probability threat whose impact would be severe: a key supplier may be acquired and deliveries interrupted. What does proper risk practice require?
options: Accept the risk without action, since low probability means it likely never occurs
Switch suppliers immediately to eliminate the threat entirely
Record it in the register with a planned response and an assigned owner
Set aside contingency equal to the full impact amount, to be safe
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Record it in the register with a planned response and an assigned owner
knowledge_checks.explanation: Severity keeps low-probability risks in scope: a documented response (like qualifying an alternate supplier) and a named owner make it managed. Passive acceptance ignores the impact half; immediate switching pays a certain cost to dodge an uncertain event; and reserving the full impact ties up funds without reducing the risk.

knowledge_checks.question: A retrospective reveals team members knew of several risks that never reached the register — no one ever asked them. Beyond adding those risks, what needs to change?
options: Nothing further — the register is now complete with the missing risks added
Risk identification should move entirely to the team, without PM involvement
Risk identification should require written submissions before every meeting
The identification process itself — make it regular and actively inclusive of team input
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: The identification process itself — make it regular and actively inclusive of team input
knowledge_checks.explanation: The gap was systemic — a process that never solicited the team — so patching the found risks without fixing the process guarantees recurrence. Removing the PM swaps one exclusion for another (their cross-stakeholder view matters), and mandatory paperwork is a mechanism, not the fix itself.

title: Risk Analysis and Response Strategies
learning_objectives: Distinguish qualitative from quantitative analysis and when each applies.
Compute and interpret expected monetary value (EMV).
Select threat and opportunity response strategies, and track secondary and residual risk.

sections.title: Qualitative, Then Quantitative
sections.content:
**Qualitative analysis** rates each risk's probability and impact on scales, producing a prioritized list — fast, always done, and enough for most risks. **Quantitative analysis** models the priority risks numerically — **EMV** (probability × impact in money), decision trees, simulation — and is reserved for where the numbers justify the effort.

**EMV** example: a 30% chance of a $200k loss carries EMV = 0.3 × (−$200k) = −$60k. Summing EMVs across identified risks is a standard way to size the contingency reserve. A **decision tree** compares options by rolling up each branch's EMV: option cost plus the probability-weighted outcomes along its path — the branch with the best net EMV wins on expected value.

sections.title: Response Strategies
sections.content:
Threat strategies: **avoid** (eliminate the risk by changing the plan), **transfer** (shift impact to a third party — insurance, fixed-price contracts; the risk itself doesn't vanish), **mitigate** (reduce probability or impact), **accept** (live with it — actively, with contingency, or passively), and **escalate** (it belongs above the project — hand it to the right owner).

Opportunity mirrors: **exploit** (make it certain), **share** (partner to capture it), **enhance** (raise probability or impact), **accept**, **escalate**. Strategy selection weighs cost against exposure: spending $150k to mitigate a −$60k EMV risk needs a better justification than arithmetic.

sections.title: Responses Create and Leave Risk
sections.content:
A response can generate a **secondary risk** (new risk caused by the response itself — the alternate supplier you qualified has its own failure modes) and almost always leaves **residual risk** (what remains after the response). Both belong in the register.

Response plans are living artifacts: a plan whose mitigation cost was estimated pre-inflation may leave the contingency undersized; a plan that depends on a departed expert is no longer executable; and a risk that partially materializes in between best and worst case needs reassessment of what remains — not mechanical execution of the original worst-case play, and not premature closure.

sections.title: Exam Perspective
sections.content: EMV arithmetic is directly tested (probability × monetary impact; decision trees roll branches up). Strategy classification too: insurance is transfer; changing the route to eliminate exposure is avoid; a partner joint venture on an opportunity is share. And the maintenance traps — stale cost estimates, orphaned response plans, partial materialization — all resolve to 'reassess and update the register,' never 'the plan said so.'

exam_traps: Confusing transfer with elimination — insurance moves the financial impact, not the event.
Forgetting responses generate secondary risks and leave residual risk — both get registered.
Executing an outdated response plan mechanically after conditions or people changed.

flashcards.definition: Probability × monetary impact — sums size contingency; decision trees compare options.
Shifting a threat's impact to a third party (insurance, contract terms) — the event itself remains possible.
A new risk created by executing a risk response.
The risk remaining after the response is applied.

knowledge_checks.question: A risk has a 20% probability of causing a $150,000 loss. What is its expected monetary value?
options: −$150,000 — the full impact is what matters for reserves
−$30,000 — probability times impact
−$75,000 — half the impact, since occurrence is uncertain
$0 — EMV applies only to opportunities, not threats
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: −$30,000 — probability times impact
knowledge_checks.explanation: EMV = 0.20 × (−$150,000) = −$30,000 — the probability-weighted exposure used for reserve sizing and option comparison. Full impact ignores probability, halving is arbitrary, and EMV applies to threats (negative) and opportunities (positive) alike.

knowledge_checks.question: The project buys insurance covering a possible equipment loss during transport. Which threat strategy is this, and what remains true?
options: Avoid — the transport threat has been fully eliminated from the project
Mitigate — the probability of the equipment loss has been meaningfully reduced
Transfer — the financial impact shifts to the insurer; the event can still occur
Accept — the project has simply chosen to live with the risk unchanged
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Transfer — the financial impact shifts to the insurer; the event can still occur
knowledge_checks.explanation: Insurance is the canonical transfer: a third party absorbs the financial consequence for a premium. The equipment can still be lost — schedule impact and disruption remain — so nothing was avoided or made less probable, and paying premiums is not acceptance.

knowledge_checks.question: To mitigate a supplier risk, the team qualifies an alternate supplier — who, the team notes, has occasional quality problems of their own. What is that new exposure called?
options: Residual risk — what remains of the original risk after response
A secondary risk — a new risk created by the response itself
An issue — since the alternate supplier's problems are already known
An assumption — the team assumes the alternate will perform adequately
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: A secondary risk — a new risk created by the response itself
knowledge_checks.explanation: The alternate supplier's quality exposure didn't exist until the response introduced that supplier — the definition of secondary risk, and it belongs in the register. Residual risk is the leftover of the original risk; nothing has occurred yet, so it isn't an issue; and known-possible quality problems are a risk, not a planning assumption.

title: Monitoring Risks and Managing Issues

eco_mappings.task: Task 4: Remove impediments and manage issues

learning_objectives: Keep risk monitoring alive: reassessment, audits, and honest register maintenance.
Run issue management with ownership, resolution dates, and root-cause capture.
Remove impediments actively, including cross-team and systemic blockers.

sections.title: Monitoring Keeps Risk Management Honest
sections.content:
After planning, risk work becomes vigilance: watching trigger conditions, reassessing as conditions change, and auditing whether the process itself is functioning. The tell of dead monitoring is a register that never changes — the same five risks, unchanged for months, is far more likely abandonment than stability, and worth checking directly.

When a risk materializes, it crosses the line: it stops being a risk and becomes an **issue** — ownership, resolution planning, active tracking. Closing it as 'no longer a risk' or leaving it in the register as a future possibility both mismanage a present reality.

sections.title: Issue Management Is Ownership
sections.content:
An **issue log** entry that manages carries an **owner**, a **target resolution date**, and status that moves. Visibility without ownership is the classic failure: an issue mentioned in four consecutive status reports with no owner and no date isn't being managed — it's being watched. Escalation has criteria too: issues genuinely beyond the team's authority go up promptly; routine issues escalated reflexively teach leadership to stop listening and teach the team it isn't trusted.

Closure includes learning: recording the root cause and resolution so a recurrence is faster to handle — or prevented outright by feeding continuous improvement.

sections.title: Impediment Removal Is Active Work
sections.content:
Some blockers can't be resolved by the people they block: shared-resource conflicts between teams, another division's queue, an organizational bottleneck recurring across projects. The project manager owns coordination: taking initiative on cross-team fixes (a shared schedule, escalation for capacity), building interim workarounds when the root fix has no timeline, and raising *patterns* — the same impediment across multiple projects — to portfolio or organizational leadership, quantified so the case is concrete.

Waiting for the affected parties to self-organize, logging without follow-through, and absorbing recurring disruption indefinitely are the anti-patterns.

sections.title: Exam Perspective
sections.content: Classification first: materialized risk → issue management. Then discipline: log + owner + date (not just visibility), escalation by criteria (not reflex), root cause on closure. For impediments: coordinate actively across boundaries, quantify impact when advocating for systemic fixes, and never let 'outside my authority' become 'not my problem' — influence and escalation paths exist precisely for that gap.

exam_traps: Reading a long-unchanged risk register as stability instead of checking whether monitoring died.
Treating status-report mentions as issue management while no owner or date exists.
Tolerating a recurring impediment indefinitely because its root fix sits in another team's queue.

flashcards.definition: The record of present problems — each with owner, target resolution date, and moving status.
An interim measure reducing an impediment's impact while the root cause awaits its fix.
A check on whether the risk management process itself is being performed and effective.

knowledge_checks.question: An issue logged three weeks ago has no owner and no target date, though it appears in every weekly status report. What is the accurate assessment?
options: It isn't being managed — visibility in reports is not ownership or a resolution path
It is being appropriately monitored through the regular reporting cycle
It must be low priority, or an owner would have volunteered by now
Status mention satisfies issue management for issues below a size threshold
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: It isn't being managed — visibility in reports is not ownership or a resolution path
knowledge_checks.explanation: Management means someone accountable and a date to hit — reports provide visibility, not action. Absence of a volunteer signals a process gap, not low priority, and no size threshold exempts an issue from having an owner.

knowledge_checks.question: A shared testing environment keeps double-booking two teams; both report it in different meetings, but neither owns the conflict. What does impediment removal require of the project manager?
options: Wait for the two teams to negotiate a schedule between themselves
Log the conflict in the issue register, since it has been verbally reported
Take ownership of coordinating a resolution — a shared schedule or escalation for capacity
Advise the blocked team to rely on local testing workarounds indefinitely
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Take ownership of coordinating a resolution — a shared schedule or escalation for capacity
knowledge_checks.explanation: A blocker spanning two teams with no natural owner is exactly what PM coordination exists for — and 'waiting for self-organization' has already demonstrably failed. Logging without follow-through is visibility again, and an indefinite degraded workaround accepts the impact without pursuing the fix.

knowledge_checks.question: The same slow legal-review turnaround has been raised as an issue on several projects across the organization. Beyond fixing it locally, what does the pattern warrant?
options: Raising the quantified cross-project pattern to portfolio or organizational leadership
Nothing — each project should continue resolving its own instance independently
Avoiding mention of the pattern, since it could read as criticizing the legal team
Assuming other project managers will independently escalate their own instances
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Raising the quantified cross-project pattern to portfolio or organizational leadership
knowledge_checks.explanation: A recurring cross-project impediment is a systemic bottleneck needing a structural fix only broader leadership can make — and quantified impact makes the case concrete rather than complaint-shaped. Instance-by-instance handling never connects the dots, silence protects the problem, and 'someone else will raise it' is how patterns stay invisible.

title: Agile Values, Principles, and Mindset
learning_objectives: State the four Agile Manifesto value pairs and read them correctly ('over', not 'instead of').
Apply the recurring principle themes: early value, feedback loops, sustainable pace, simplicity.
Distinguish genuine agile mindset from ceremony-only 'agile theater.'

sections.title: Four Values, Read Carefully
sections.content:
The Agile Manifesto weighs four pairs: **individuals and interactions** over processes and tools; **working product** over comprehensive documentation; **customer collaboration** over contract negotiation; **responding to change** over following a plan.

The word 'over' does the work: the right-hand items retain value — agile teams still document, plan, and contract — but when the pairs conflict, the left side wins. Reading the values as 'no documentation, no plans' is the most common corruption, and exam distractors exploit it.

sections.title: Principle Themes
sections.content:
The twelve principles cluster into themes worth internalizing rather than memorizing verbatim: **deliver value early and continuously** (working product is the primary progress measure); **welcome change**, even late, because responsiveness is competitive advantage; **short feedback loops** — frequent delivery, daily business-developer collaboration, regular reflection (retrospectives) with behavior adjustment; **empowered, self-organizing teams** given environment and trust; **sustainable pace** indefinitely, because burnout pace is borrowed speed; and **simplicity** — maximizing work *not* done.

Each theme is a decision lens: given options, the agile-credited one shortens feedback, delivers value sooner, or preserves team health.

sections.title: Mindset vs. Theater
sections.content: Agile is a mindset expressed through practices — not the practices alone. A team holding daily standups while decisions still flow top-down, or running 'sprints' whose content is fixed a year ahead, has adopted ceremony without the mindset: **agile theater**. The diagnostic questions: Does customer feedback actually change the backlog? Do retrospectives actually change behavior? Can the team actually make decisions inside its boundary? When those answers are no, the fix is never more ceremony — it's restoring the feedback and empowerment loops the ceremonies exist to serve.

sections.title: Exam Perspective
sections.content: Value-pair questions test the 'over' reading — documentation and planning are reduced-emphasis, not eliminated. Scenario questions reward shortening feedback loops, welcoming (channeled) change, and protecting sustainable pace. And when a scenario shows ceremonies running but outcomes unchanged, the credited diagnosis is the missing mindset element, not a missing meeting.

exam_traps: Reading 'over' as 'instead of' — agile still documents, plans, and contracts.
Prescribing more ceremony when the missing ingredient is feedback or empowerment.
Treating sustained overtime as agile commitment — sustainable pace is a principle, not a suggestion.

flashcards.definition: Individuals/interactions, working product, customer collaboration, responding to change — each 'over' (not 'instead of') its pair.
A work rate maintainable indefinitely — the principle burnout-driven schedules violate.
Ceremonies without the mindset — standups and sprints with no real feedback or empowerment loops.

knowledge_checks.question: A stakeholder objects: 'Agile means no documentation, so we can't use it — we're regulated.' What is the accurate correction?
options: Required documentation still gets produced — the value only weights conflicts
The stakeholder is right — agile and regulated environments are incompatible
Agile teams produce their documentation only after the product has shipped
Documentation in agile becomes the product owner's personal responsibility
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Required documentation still gets produced — the value only weights conflicts
knowledge_checks.explanation: The value statement weights priorities when they conflict; it doesn't abolish the right-hand side. Regulated agile teams build required documentation into their definition of done. Incompatibility is the myth being corrected, and neither deferral-until-shipping nor PO-only ownership is an agile rule.

knowledge_checks.question: A team runs every Scrum ceremony faithfully, but customer feedback never changes the backlog and retrospective actions never get done. What is the credited diagnosis?
options: The ceremonies need to run longer and more frequently to finally take effect
The team has clearly outgrown agile and should return to predictive delivery
Agile theater — the ceremonies run, but the loops they serve are dead
The customer is at fault for giving feedback the team cannot readily use
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Agile theater — the ceremonies run, but the loops they serve are dead
knowledge_checks.explanation: Ceremonies are delivery mechanisms for feedback and adaptation; running them while ignoring their outputs is form without function. More ceremony amplifies the theater, abandoning agile misdiagnoses practice failure as approach failure, and blaming the customer deflects from loops the team controls.

knowledge_checks.question: Which option best expresses the 'simplicity' principle in day-to-day work?
options: Building every requested feature to its most complete possible version
Maximizing the amount of work not done — building only what the outcome needs
Removing code comments and documentation to reduce artifact count
Refusing stakeholder requests that would add any complexity at all
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Maximizing the amount of work not done — building only what the outcome needs
knowledge_checks.explanation: Simplicity is the art of not building what the outcome doesn't need — the manifesto's own phrasing. Gold-plating every feature is its opposite; stripping useful documentation confuses simplicity with austerity; and blanket refusal of complexity ignores that some complexity earns its keep.

title: Scrum: Roles, Events, Artifacts, and Pillars
learning_objectives: Assign responsibilities correctly across Product Owner, Scrum Master, and Developers.
Explain each Scrum event's purpose and timebox logic.
Connect the three artifacts to their commitments and the three empirical pillars.

sections.title: Three Accountabilities
sections.content:
**Product Owner**: owns the *what* — maximizes product value, owns and orders the product backlog, is one person (not a committee), and is the single channel for what the team builds next. Stakeholders influence; the PO decides. **Scrum Master**: owns the *how the system works* — a servant leader who coaches the team, facilitates events, removes impediments, and protects the team from disruption; explicitly *not* a taskmaster or the team's boss. **Developers**: own the *how* — everyone building the increment, regardless of specialty; they self-organize, own the sprint backlog, and decide how much work fits a sprint.

The boundary questions are exam staples: prioritization belongs to the PO (not the SM, not the sponsor, not the loudest stakeholder); sprint workload belongs to the Developers; impediment removal and process health belong to the SM.

sections.title: Five Events
sections.content:
The **Sprint** is the container — a fixed timebox (a month or less) producing a done, usable increment. **Sprint Planning** starts it: the team selects backlog items and crafts a sprint goal. **Daily Scrum**: 15 minutes, by and for the Developers, to inspect progress toward the sprint goal and adapt the day's plan — not a status report to management. **Sprint Review**: inspect the increment with stakeholders, gather feedback that updates the backlog. **Sprint Retrospective**: the team inspects its own process and commits to concrete improvements.

Timeboxes are protective: fixed length forces prioritization and guarantees the feedback cadence.

sections.title: Artifacts, Commitments, Pillars
sections.content:
Three artifacts, each with a commitment: the **product backlog** (commitment: the *product goal*), the **sprint backlog** (commitment: the *sprint goal*), and the **increment** (commitment: the *definition of done*). An increment that doesn't meet the definition of done isn't an increment — it's inventory.

Beneath everything sit the empirical pillars: **transparency** (real state visible to those doing and receiving the work), **inspection** (frequent examination of artifacts and progress), and **adaptation** (adjusting when inspection finds deviation). Every event and artifact is a transparency-inspection-adaptation mechanism; that's the theory to reason from when a practice question gets novel.

sections.title: Exam Perspective
sections.content: Role-boundary scenarios dominate: a sponsor pushing items directly to the team (route through the PO), a Scrum Master assigning tasks (not their role — Developers self-organize), a stakeholder demanding mid-sprint additions (backlog, next sprint, PO's call). Event-purpose questions reward knowing the Daily Scrum belongs to the Developers and the Retrospective inspects process, not product — that's the Review's job.

exam_traps: Letting anyone but the Product Owner order the product backlog.
Treating the Daily Scrum as a status meeting for managers rather than the Developers' planning huddle.
Confusing the Sprint Review (inspect the product) with the Retrospective (inspect the process).

flashcards.definition: One person owning product value and backlog order — the single channel for what gets built.
Servant leader coaching the team, facilitating events, and removing impediments — not a taskmaster.
The increment's commitment — the quality bar that makes work genuinely complete.
Transparency, inspection, adaptation — the theory every Scrum event and artifact serves.

knowledge_checks.question: A sponsor walks directly to two developers and asks them to build a 'quick feature' this sprint. In Scrum, what is the correct routing?
options: The developers build it, since sponsors outrank the backlog
The Scrum Master decides whether the feature enters the sprint
The request goes to the Product Owner for backlog prioritization
The developers vote as a team on whether to accept the work
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: The request goes to the Product Owner for backlog prioritization
knowledge_checks.explanation: The Product Owner is the single channel for what gets built — stakeholder requests, sponsor requests included, enter through the backlog they order. Sponsor rank doesn't bypass the role, the Scrum Master owns process rather than product decisions, and team votes don't replace the PO's accountability for value.

knowledge_checks.question: During the Daily Scrum, a department manager attends and asks each developer to justify yesterday's hours. What has gone wrong?
options: Nothing — the Daily Scrum exists precisely for management status checks
The event was repurposed — it belongs to the Developers' daily planning
The meeting exceeded its fifteen-minute timebox, which is the only real rule
The manager should have asked the Product Owner to collect the justifications
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: The event was repurposed — it belongs to the Developers' daily planning
knowledge_checks.explanation: The Daily Scrum is the Developers' 15-minute inspection-and-adaptation huddle, not an accountability report to management — converting it into one damages the transparency it needs to work. The timebox isn't the issue here, and routing interrogation through the PO just relocates the dysfunction.

knowledge_checks.question: At sprint end, an item works in the demo environment but hasn't passed the security review the definition of done requires. What is its status?
options: Done — a working demonstration is the practical meaning of an increment
Done, provided the Product Owner accepts the demonstration as sufficient
Not done — the definition of done is a commitment, and it is not met
Done at reduced story points, to reflect the missing security review
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Not done — the definition of done is a commitment, and it is not met
knowledge_checks.explanation: The definition of done is a hard quality bar — work that misses any element isn't an increment, whatever the demo shows. The PO can't waive it item-by-item without corrupting the standard, and 'partial credit' scoring hides undone work inside velocity.

title: Kanban, Lean, and Flow
learning_objectives: Apply Kanban's core practices: visualize work, limit WIP, manage flow.
Interpret flow metrics: lead time, cycle time, throughput, and Little's Law.
Connect Lean thinking — waste elimination, pull, optimize the whole — to project work.

sections.title: Kanban's Core Moves
sections.content:
**Kanban** manages work through flow rather than timeboxes. The core practices: **visualize the workflow** (a board where every work item and its state is visible — invisible work can't be managed), **limit work in progress** (explicit WIP caps per state), and **manage flow** (watch where work piles up, fix the bottleneck).

WIP limits are the counterintuitive engine: capping simultaneous work *increases* completion rate, because task-switching and queue-aging are real costs. When a column hits its limit, the team swarms to finish existing work instead of starting more — 'stop starting, start finishing.'

sections.title: Flow Metrics
sections.content:
**Lead time**: request to delivery — the customer's clock. **Cycle time**: work started to work finished — the team's clock. **Throughput**: items completed per period. **Little's Law** ties them: average cycle time = WIP ÷ throughput — which is *why* limiting WIP shortens cycle time at a given throughput.

These metrics are empirical forecasting inputs: 'we complete roughly eight similar items a week' predicts better than optimistic per-item estimates. A swelling WIP count with flat throughput is an early warning — cycle times are about to stretch.

sections.title: Lean Thinking
sections.content:
Kanban inherits **Lean**: eliminate **waste** (partially done work, waiting, handoffs, task-switching, defects, gold-plated features nobody uses), use **pull** (work is pulled when capacity exists, not pushed onto overloaded people), and **optimize the whole** (a locally faster step that floods the next step's queue made the *system* slower).

Partially done work is the signature Lean insight for projects: ten items 80% finished deliver zero value; eight items 100% finished deliver eight items of value. Inventory of unfinished work is cost, not progress.

sections.title: Exam Perspective
sections.content: Scenario signals: a team drowning in simultaneous work → WIP limits, not more multitasking; complaints that 'everything takes forever' → measure and manage flow, find the bottleneck; a stakeholder pushing work onto a full team → pull systems and visible WIP make the overload undeniable. Kanban also fits where timeboxes don't: continuous intake work (support, operations-adjacent streams) flows better than it sprints.

exam_traps: Responding to overload by starting more work in parallel — WIP limits exist to prevent exactly this.
Confusing lead time (customer's clock, from request) with cycle time (team's clock, from start).
Optimizing one workflow step in isolation while the downstream queue swells.

flashcards.definition: An explicit cap on simultaneous work per state — finishing beats starting.
Average cycle time = WIP ÷ throughput — the math behind limiting work in progress.
Work enters when capacity exists, rather than being pushed onto loaded people.
Lead: request → delivery (customer's clock). Cycle: start → finish (team's clock).

knowledge_checks.question: A team has 24 items in progress, everything is 'almost done,' and completion has slowed to a crawl. What is the Kanban-credited intervention?
options: Start the remaining backlog items too, so nothing waits unattended
Impose WIP limits and swarm to finish existing work before starting more
Extend working hours until the parallel workload clears
Reassign the slowest items to the fastest team members
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Impose WIP limits and swarm to finish existing work before starting more
knowledge_checks.explanation: Massive parallel WIP is the disease: task-switching and aging queues stall completion. Capping WIP and finishing before starting restores flow — Little's Law makes the math explicit. Starting more work deepens the spiral, overtime burns people against a structural problem, and reshuffling assignees doesn't reduce the parallelism.

knowledge_checks.question: A customer asks why their request took six weeks when the team 'only worked on it for four days.' Which two metrics explain the gap?
options: Velocity and story points — the item was simply under-estimated at intake
Throughput and takt time — the arrival rate exceeded the service rate
Lead time (six weeks with queue wait) versus cycle time (four active days)
Burndown and burnup — the sprint scope changed during the six weeks
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Lead time (six weeks with queue wait) versus cycle time (four active days)
knowledge_checks.explanation: Lead time runs on the customer's clock from request to delivery — including the five-plus weeks in queue; cycle time covers only the active work. The gap is queue time, which WIP limits attack. Velocity, takt, and burn charts don't decompose this particular customer-facing question.

knowledge_checks.question: One workflow step gets optimized and doubles its output — and total delivery slows as the next step's queue explodes. Which Lean principle was violated?
options: Visualize the workflow — the board must have been out of date
Optimize the whole — a local improvement flooded the system's constraint
Eliminate defects — the faster step must have produced lower quality
Sustainable pace — the optimized step is working too fast to maintain
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Optimize the whole — a local improvement flooded the system's constraint
knowledge_checks.explanation: System throughput is set by the constraint; accelerating a non-constraint step just piles inventory in front of the bottleneck. That's the optimize-the-whole lesson. No evidence points to board staleness, defects, or pace problems — the queue explosion is pure local-optimization arithmetic.

title: Product Vision, Backlogs, and User Stories

eco_mappings.task: Task 2: Develop and manage project scope

learning_objectives: Trace the cascade: vision → roadmap → releases → epics → stories.
Write and evaluate user stories: format, three Cs, INVEST, acceptance criteria.
Run backlog refinement and value-based ordering honestly.

sections.title: From Vision to Story
sections.content:
Adaptive scope lives in a cascade: the **product vision** (why the product exists, for whom, what difference it makes) anchors a **roadmap** (major outcomes over time), which frames **releases**, which decompose into **epics** (large bodies of work) and finally **user stories** (small, deliverable slices of value). The cascade keeps daily work connected to purpose — a story that can't be traced upward to the vision is a candidate for not building it.

The backlog is the single, ordered list of all of it — one list, one owner (the product owner), continuously reordered by value.

sections.title: User Stories Done Right
sections.content:
The canonical format: *As a [role], I want [capability], so that [benefit]* — the 'so that' clause is the value test; when it's hollow, the story is suspect. The **three Cs** describe a story's lifecycle: **Card** (the brief written token), **Conversation** (the collaborative detail-filling — the story is a promise to talk, not a spec), and **Confirmation** (the acceptance criteria that define done for this story).

**INVEST** is the quality checklist: **I**ndependent (schedulable on its own), **N**egotiable (details flex in conversation), **V**aluable (delivers something someone wants), **E**stimable (understood well enough to size), **S**mall (fits comfortably in an iteration), **T**estable (verifiable criteria exist).

sections.title: Refinement and Ordering
sections.content:
**Backlog refinement** is the ongoing grooming that keeps the near-term backlog ready: splitting epics into stories, adding acceptance criteria, updating estimates, deleting the dead weight. Deep items get detail as they approach; distant items stay coarse — refinement is rolling wave in adaptive clothing.

Ordering is by **value**, honestly measured: cost of delay, risk reduction, learning value, and dependencies all count. The recurring corruptions: ordering by convenience (easy first), by recency (newest request first), by volume (loudest stakeholder first) — and a scoring model quietly overweighting 'easy to build' produces throughput theater, not value delivery.

sections.title: Exam Perspective
sections.content: Story-format questions test the pieces (the 'so that' carries value; acceptance criteria are the Confirmation). INVEST questions test diagnosis — a story the team can't estimate usually isn't small or well-enough understood; a story with no user benefit fails Valuable. Backlog questions reward single-list, PO-ordered, value-driven discipline against every variant of queue-jumping.

exam_traps: Treating a user story as a mini-specification instead of a placeholder for conversation.
Writing stories with hollow 'so that' clauses — activity without a value claim.
Letting refinement detail the entire backlog uniformly instead of sharpening what's near.

flashcards.definition: As a [role], I want [capability], so that [benefit] — the benefit clause carries the value test.
Card, Conversation, Confirmation — a story is a promise to talk, confirmed by acceptance criteria.
Independent, Negotiable, Valuable, Estimable, Small, Testable — the story quality checklist.

knowledge_checks.question: A story reads: 'As a claims adjuster, I want one-click access to prior claims, so that I can resolve calls faster.' Which element makes this story's VALUE testable?
options: The one-click phrasing, which specifies the interaction precisely
The claims-adjuster role, which identifies the requesting department
The 'so that I can resolve calls faster' clause — a measurable benefit claim
The story's brevity, which guarantees it fits within one iteration
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: The 'so that I can resolve calls faster' clause — a measurable benefit claim
knowledge_checks.explanation: The benefit clause states why the capability matters and gives something to validate after delivery (did resolution speed improve?). Interaction detail and role identify the what and who; brevity relates to Small, not value.

knowledge_checks.question: A developer complains a story 'can't be estimated — nobody really knows what it involves.' Which INVEST failure is this, and what's the remedy?
options: Estimable, likely Small too — refine it: split, clarify, add criteria
Independent — the story must first be merged with all of its dependencies
Negotiable — the team should simply commit and negotiate the details later
Testable — the story mainly needs a dedicated QA resource assigned to it
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Estimable, likely Small too — refine it: split, clarify, add criteria
knowledge_checks.explanation: Un-estimatable stories are usually too big or too vague — refinement (splitting, conversation, criteria) restores estimability. Merging dependencies makes stories bigger, not clearer; 'commit and figure it out' abuses Negotiable; and testability is about criteria, not staffing.

knowledge_checks.question: A backlog's top items are consistently the easiest to build, while a high-value, complex item sinks for months. What discipline has failed?
options: Sprint capacity planning — the team's velocity has been set far too low
Value-based ordering — convenience is outranking worth; the PO should fix it
Story splitting — the complex item should become forty tiny stories instead
Definition of done — the completed easy items must be hiding many defects
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Value-based ordering — convenience is outranking worth; the PO should fix it
knowledge_checks.explanation: The backlog exists to put the most valuable work next; easy-first ordering is throughput theater that defers the value the product exists for. Velocity and DoD aren't implicated by the pattern, and while splitting may help the complex item move, the ordering discipline is the failed control.

title: Agile Estimation and Forecasting
learning_objectives: Explain relative estimation: story points, planning poker, affinity sizing.
Use velocity correctly — an empirical planning input, never a performance target.
Read burndown/burnup charts and forecast from observed completion rates.

sections.title: Relative, Not Absolute
sections.content:
Agile estimation sizes work *relative to other work*: **story points** express combined effort, complexity, and uncertainty against reference stories, usually on a Fibonacci-ish scale (1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13) whose widening gaps mirror how uncertainty grows with size. Humans compare better than they measure — 'this is roughly twice that' is more reliable than 'this is 34 hours.'

**Planning poker** (simultaneous reveal, discuss the outliers, converge) exists to counter anchoring and authority bias — the point is the conversation the divergence triggers. **Affinity estimation** and **T-shirt sizing** trade precision for speed across large backlogs.

sections.title: Velocity: Input, Not Target
sections.content:
**Velocity** is the empirical average of points completed per iteration — a *planning input*: capacity for next sprint ≈ demonstrated velocity, and remaining backlog ÷ velocity ≈ remaining iterations. Two laws govern its use. First, plan from *demonstrated* velocity, not aspiration — a team that commits to 40 and completes 25, sprint after sprint, has a planning-input problem, not a work-ethic problem. Second, velocity is not comparable across teams and collapses as a performance target: pressure it, and points inflate to match (story-point inflation), destroying the measure's forecasting value.

The quiet corruption to watch: scope trimmed informally to keep a smooth burndown — status integrity beats chart aesthetics.

sections.title: Charts and Forecasts
sections.content:
A **burndown** plots remaining work against time; a **burnup** plots completed work *and* total scope — making scope growth visible instead of disguising it as slow progress. Flow-based teams forecast from **throughput** (items/week) the same way sprint teams use velocity.

Empirical forecasting states ranges honestly: 'at 20–24 points per sprint, this backlog lands in 5–6 sprints' — updated every iteration as data arrives. That's the adaptive answer to 'when will it be done': a range from evidence, tightening over time.

sections.title: Exam Perspective
sections.content: Credited patterns: plan next sprint from demonstrated velocity; investigate persistent commit-vs-complete gaps as estimation/planning problems; prefer burnup when scope is moving; forecast in evidence-based ranges. Distractors: velocity as a whip, cross-team velocity comparisons, hour-precision promises from point-based data, and burndowns smoothed by quiet scope trimming.

exam_traps: Planning sprints from hoped-for velocity while demonstrated velocity says otherwise.
Comparing velocities across teams or setting velocity as a performance target.
Reading a smooth burndown as health when scope is being quietly trimmed to produce it.

flashcards.definition: Relative size — effort, complexity, uncertainty — measured against reference stories.
Demonstrated points per iteration — a planning input, never a cross-team metric or target.
Completed work plotted with total scope — makes scope growth visible.

knowledge_checks.question: A team commits to 40 points every sprint and completes about 25, sprint after sprint. What is the credited correction?
options: Plan future sprints from the demonstrated ~25-point velocity, not the aspiration
Apply schedule pressure until completed points reach the 40-point commitment
Lengthen the sprint so the 40 points fit within the timebox
Add team members until capacity matches the 40-point plan
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Plan future sprints from the demonstrated ~25-point velocity, not the aspiration
knowledge_checks.explanation: Persistent commit-complete gaps are planning-input errors: the evidence says 25. Pressure produces point inflation or burnout, longer sprints just relabel the same throughput, and adding people first raises coordination costs — none of them fix planning against fiction.

knowledge_checks.question: Leadership wants to compare two teams' velocities to decide which is 'more productive.' What is wrong with this?
options: Nothing — velocity exists precisely for cross-team productivity benchmarking
Points are team-relative; comparison invites inflation and ruins forecasts
Velocity comparisons are fine as long as both teams use Fibonacci numbers
The comparison becomes valid only after normalizing by each team's size
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Points are team-relative; comparison invites inflation and ruins forecasts
knowledge_checks.explanation: Each team's points are calibrated to its own reference stories — the units differ. Comparing them punishes honest calibration, and teams respond by inflating estimates, wrecking the planning signal. Shared scales and size normalization don't fix incommensurable units.

knowledge_checks.question: Stakeholders keep adding scope, and the burndown chart makes the team look slow even though completion is steady. Which chart fixes the visibility problem?
options: A burnup chart — completed work and total scope plotted separately
A steeper ideal-line burndown to reset stakeholder expectations
A cumulative defect chart to shift attention toward quality
No chart change — the team should simply complete the added scope faster
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: A burnup chart — completed work and total scope plotted separately
knowledge_checks.explanation: Burnups separate the two moving quantities: the completed line shows steady delivery while the scope line shows growth — exactly the story the burndown collapses into apparent slowness. Cosmetic ideal lines and topic changes don't restore the signal, and 'work faster' accepts a distorted picture.

title: MVP, MMF, and Early Value
learning_objectives: Distinguish MVP (learning instrument) from MMF (value instrument).
Apply cost of delay to sequencing decisions.
Recognize when 'minimum' is being misused as 'sloppy' or 'everything.'

sections.title: MVP: Built to Learn
sections.content:
A **minimum viable product** is the smallest thing that tests the riskiest assumption — its output is *validated learning*, not revenue. 'Will adjusters actually use self-service?' is answered faster by a thin, real slice in front of real users than by a year of building on the untested assumption.

Minimum modifies scope, not quality: an MVP is a small *good* thing, not a large sloppy one. And the definition is a negotiation hazard — stakeholders each defining 'minimum' as 'everything I want' produces an MVP the size of the product. When two parties hold different mental models of the MVP, that's an alignment conversation to have explicitly, early.

sections.title: MMF: Built to Earn
sections.content:
