A **minimum marketable feature** is the smallest feature chunk that delivers real, standalone value to customers — the unit of incremental *delivery*, where MVP is the unit of *learning*. A payments product might ship card support as an MMF (customers can genuinely use it) while running an MVP experiment on whether invoice financing is worth building at all.

The sequencing logic both serve: value lands earlier, feedback arrives while change is cheap, and risk falls with each real release.

sections.title: Cost of Delay
sections.content:
**Cost of delay** prices time: what does each month of not shipping this item cost — in revenue missed, savings deferred, risk carried, deadlines (regulatory!) approached? It converts sequencing debates into economics: a feature worth $100k/month of delay outranks one worth $10k/month even if the second is easier — and 'easier first' is exactly the corruption cost-of-delay thinking exposes.

Compliance deadlines are cost-of-delay cliffs: the cost isn't linear, it's a wall, which is why compliance-mandatory work jumps queues.

sections.title: Exam Perspective
sections.content: Definition questions: MVP tests assumptions (learning); MMF delivers standalone value (earning); neither means low quality. Scenario questions: divergent MVP definitions between stakeholders → align explicitly before building; sequencing under time pressure → compliance-mandatory and highest cost-of-delay first; and a 'minimum' that keeps absorbing everyone's wishlist → the scoping conversation, not silent acceptance.

exam_traps: Reading 'minimum' as permission for low quality rather than small scope.
Using MVP and MMF interchangeably — one tests assumptions, the other delivers value.
Sequencing by ease while cost of delay points the other way.

flashcards.definition: The smallest thing that tests the riskiest assumption — output is validated learning.
The smallest feature delivering real standalone customer value — the unit of incremental delivery.
What each period of not shipping an item costs — the economics behind sequencing.

knowledge_checks.question: A team wants to test whether customers would actually use a proposed self-service portal before investing a year in it. Which instrument fits, and what is its output?
options: An MMF — the smallest marketable version, with revenue as the output
An MVP — a thin real slice for real users, yielding validated learning
A prototype review board, with formal approval sign-off as the output
A full pilot release across the market, with share growth as the output
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: An MVP — a thin real slice for real users, yielding validated learning
knowledge_checks.explanation: The question is an assumption test — will they use it? — which is the MVP's exact job, and its yield is learning that steers the investment decision. MMFs deliver value once the direction is validated; approval boards test opinions, not behavior; and a full pilot spends the year the MVP exists to protect.

knowledge_checks.question: A product owner and a key customer each say 'MVP' but describe wildly different feature sets. What is the credited move?
options: Build the union of both descriptions so neither party is disappointed
Build the intersection of both descriptions, since that must be the true minimum
Surface the divergence and explicitly align on what the MVP tests and contains
Proceed with the product owner's version, since they own the backlog
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Surface the divergence and explicitly align on what the MVP tests and contains
knowledge_checks.explanation: Different mental models of 'minimum' are an alignment gap that grows more expensive with every sprint built on it. The union is the everything-MVP corruption, the intersection is an arbitrary artifact of two wishlists, and PO authority doesn't remove the need to reconcile with a key customer before building.

knowledge_checks.question: Feature A saves $80k per month once shipped; Feature B saves $15k per month but is much easier to build. Cost-of-delay reasoning says?
options: Ship A first — each month of delay on A costs over five times more
Ship B first — quick wins build momentum for the harder work
Ship both simultaneously by splitting the team down the middle
Ship neither until a full year's roadmap is finalized and approved
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Ship A first — each month of delay on A costs over five times more
knowledge_checks.explanation: Cost of delay prices the queue: every month B jumps ahead of A burns ~$65k net. 'Momentum' is the easy-first rationalization the economics exist to expose, splitting the team delays the high-value item anyway, and waiting for a perfect roadmap maximizes the delay cost on both.

title: Servant Leadership and Self-Organizing Teams
learning_objectives: Describe servant leadership behaviors: remove obstacles, shield, grow people.
Explain self-organization as bounded authority over the how.
Diagnose the failure modes: command-and-control relapse and abdication.

sections.title: The Leader Serves the Team
sections.content:
**Servant leadership** inverts the traditional flow: instead of the team serving the leader's plan, the leader serves the team's ability to deliver — **removing impediments** (the blocked environment, the stalled dependency, the bureaucratic snag), **shielding** the team from disruption (scope injections, drive-by tasking, stakeholder interrupts routed properly instead of landing mid-sprint), and **growing people** (coaching, creating stretch opportunities, building safety to fail and learn).

The posture is 'what's blocking you and how do I clear it?' — asked genuinely and acted on. Influence and facilitation, not command, are the working tools.

sections.title: Self-Organization Is Bounded
sections.content:
A **self-organizing team** decides *how* to do the work: task assignment, technical approach, internal coordination. It operates inside boundaries: the *what and why* come from product ownership and the vision; standards and constraints (quality bars, compliance) still apply. Self-organization is authority over method, not anarchy over goals.

It also has prerequisites — clear goals, sufficient skills, access to information, and trust. Handing a team 'self-organization' without goals or context isn't empowerment; it's abdication with better branding.

sections.title: Two Failure Modes
sections.content:
**Command-and-control relapse**: the leader assigns tasks person by person, re-decides team decisions, or converts the daily standup into status interrogation — the ceremonies survive, the self-organization dies, and the team learns to wait for instructions. **Abdication**: 'you're self-organizing' as an excuse to withhold direction, feedback, and impediment removal — the team flails without the context and support self-organization requires.

The healthy middle: crystal-clear outcomes and boundaries, genuine authority within them, active service on obstacles, and coaching — not commanding, not vanishing.

sections.title: Exam Perspective
sections.content: Servant-leader scenarios reward clearing the blocker over reporting it, shielding the team over relaying every interrupt, and coaching over commanding. Self-organization scenarios reward letting the team own task assignment and method — while keeping goals, priorities, and standards explicit. The distractors are the two failure modes wearing reasonable-sounding clothes: 'ensuring quality' (micromanagement) and 'trusting the team' (abdication).

exam_traps: Reporting impediments upward while the team stays blocked — servant leaders clear paths.
Assigning tasks to individuals on a team that should own its own task distribution.
Using 'self-organization' to justify withholding direction, context, or support.

flashcards.definition: Leading by serving the team's ability to deliver — clearing obstacles, shielding, growing people.
A team with authority over how work gets done — inside explicit goals, priorities, and standards.
Routing disruptions (scope injections, drive-by requests) properly so the team's focus survives.

knowledge_checks.question: A team is blocked by another department's approval queue. The servant-leader response is:
options: Note the blockage in the risk register and wait for the queue to clear itself
Work the impediment actively — engage the department, escalate if needed
Tell the team to quietly route around the approval requirement this once
Reassign the blocked team members to entirely unrelated work indefinitely
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Work the impediment actively — engage the department, escalate if needed
knowledge_checks.explanation: Impediment removal is the servant leader's core service: engage the blocker directly, escalate with quantified impact if direct engagement stalls. Logging-and-waiting is visibility without service, bypassing approvals trades one problem for a compliance problem, and permanent reassignment abandons the blocked work.

knowledge_checks.question: A newly 'self-organizing' team flounders: no clear goals, no priorities, and the leader has stepped entirely away 'to empower them.' What is the actual failure?
options: Self-organization itself — the model simply doesn't work for most teams
The team's skill mix — self-organization requires senior engineers only
Abdication — goals, context, and active support were withheld
Insufficient ceremonies — daily standups would supply the missing direction
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Abdication — goals, context, and active support were withheld
knowledge_checks.explanation: Self-organization governs the how; the what and why must still be supplied, along with coaching and impediment service. Stepping away entirely is abdication branded as empowerment. The model isn't disproven by its prerequisites being unmet, seniority isn't the gate, and ceremonies can't substitute for missing goals.

knowledge_checks.question: A project manager on an agile team assigns each developer their tasks daily 'to keep things efficient.' What does this practice cost?
options: Nothing — task assignment is a core project management duty everywhere
Only some PM time that could be delegated to the Scrum Master instead
A minor ceremony violation carrying no practical delivery consequence
The team's self-organization — task ownership keeps commitment with the team
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: The team's self-organization — task ownership keeps commitment with the team
knowledge_checks.explanation: Daily task assignment relocates the how from the team to the leader: engagement drops to compliance, local knowledge stops informing distribution, and people wait to be told. It isn't universal PM duty in adaptive settings, delegating the controlling behavior doesn't fix it, and the consequence is practical, not ceremonial.

title: Hybrid Integration in Practice

eco_mappings.task: Task 1: Develop an integrated project management plan and plan delivery

learning_objectives: Assign the right delivery mode per component within one project.
Wire the seams: reporting translation, dual change regimes, cadence connection.
Manage the cultural integration between plan-driven and adaptive groups.

sections.title: Mode per Component
sections.content:
Hybrid's practical core is assigning delivery mode by component characteristics within a single project: the compliance module with fixed regulatory requirements runs predictive (baselined, formally controlled); the user-experience layer with high uncertainty runs adaptive (backlog, sprints, feedback); the infrastructure build with long lead times runs predictive with phased milestones.

The assignment follows the same tailoring factors as whole projects — uncertainty, feedback availability, cost of change, compliance demands — applied at component grain. What never works: assigning mode by team preference and hoping the seams sort themselves out.

sections.title: Wiring the Seams
sections.content: The seams are where hybrid succeeds or dies. **Reporting**: sprint burnups and milestone variance must translate into one leadership picture — define the translation once (e.g., sprint outputs mapped to milestone progress) rather than forcing either side to fake the other's metrics. **Change**: each piece of work belongs to exactly one regime — baselined components go through change control, backlog components through the product owner — and cross-boundary changes (an adaptive discovery that moves a predictive interface) trigger *both*: impact analysis where baselines move, reprioritization where backlogs do. **Cadence**: sprint reviews feed gate reviews; gate decisions feed backlog priorities; dependencies between a sprinting team and a milestone-driven team get explicit synchronization points, not vibes.

sections.title: Cultural Integration
sections.content: Hybrid also merges working cultures: the predictive side reads the adaptive side as undisciplined ('no commitments!'), the adaptive side reads the predictive side as bureaucratic ('gates everywhere!') — both misreadings of different-but-real discipline. The integration work is mutual literacy (each side understanding what the other's controls protect), shared definitions where the modes meet ('done,' 'approved,' 'committed' mean the same thing at the interface), and leadership that visibly values both disciplines rather than declaring one culture the winner.

sections.title: Exam Perspective
sections.content: Component-mode questions apply tailoring factors at component level. Seam questions reward: one change regime per work item with cross-boundary changes triggering both processes; honest reporting translation; explicit sync points for cross-mode dependencies. Culture questions reward mutual literacy and shared interface definitions over forcing uniformity in either direction.

exam_traps: Letting a work item answer to two change regimes — or to none — at the same time.
Forcing one side's metrics onto the other instead of defining an honest translation.
Treating the other mode's discipline as dysfunction instead of different protection.

flashcards.definition: Assigning delivery mode per component by its uncertainty, feedback, change-cost, and compliance profile.
An adaptive discovery that moves baselined work (or vice versa) — triggers both change processes.
An explicit meeting of cadences where sprint outputs and milestone gates exchange decisions.

knowledge_checks.question: In a hybrid project, an adaptive team's discovery requires changing an interface owned by the predictive workstream's baseline. What does the change require?
options: Both processes: change control for the baseline, backlog reprioritization for the adaptive side
Only backlog reprioritization, since the discovery originated in the adaptive workstream
Only a courtesy notification sent to the predictive team after the implementation
Nothing formal at all — hybrid projects exist to eliminate change ceremony entirely
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Both processes: change control for the baseline, backlog reprioritization for the adaptive side
knowledge_checks.explanation: The change crosses the regime boundary: baselined work moves only through change control, and the adaptive side's plans reorder through its backlog. Origin doesn't exempt the baseline, after-the-fact notification bypasses control entirely, and hybrid combines disciplines rather than dissolving them.

knowledge_checks.question: Leadership complains they can't see one coherent status picture: half the project reports burnups, half reports milestone variance. What is the credited fix?
options: Require the adaptive teams to convert entirely to milestone-variance reporting
Require the predictive teams to convert entirely to burnup-style reporting
Define a translation once — sprint outputs mapped into one integrated view
Send leadership both raw formats and let them integrate the picture mentally
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Define a translation once — sprint outputs mapped into one integrated view
knowledge_checks.explanation: Each mode's native metrics carry its real signal; forcing either side to fake the other's numbers degrades both. The integration work is a defined translation into one leadership picture. Dumping raw duality on leadership is the complaint, not the cure.

knowledge_checks.question: The predictive-side lead calls the agile teams 'undisciplined'; the agile lead calls the gates 'pointless bureaucracy.' What does hybrid integration actually require here?
options: Declaring one culture the standard and converting the other side over to it
Keeping the two groups permanently separated so the friction cannot surface
Mutual literacy about what each side's controls protect, plus shared interface definitions
Escalating the culture clash to human resources as an interpersonal conflict
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Mutual literacy about what each side's controls protect, plus shared interface definitions
knowledge_checks.explanation: Both perceptions misread genuine discipline as dysfunction; the fix is understanding (backlogs and timeboxes are control; gates protect investment decisions) plus agreed interface meanings for 'done' and 'approved.' Forced conversion loses one mode's real protections, separation entrenches the stereotypes, and HR escalation treats a structural literacy gap as a personality problem.

title: AI Foundations for Project Managers

eco_mappings.task: Task 8: Evaluate external business environment changes

learning_objectives: Define the core vocabulary: AI, machine learning, generative AI, large language models.
Explain what these systems fundamentally do — and the failure modes that follow.
Position AI as decision support under human judgment, never autonomous authority.

sections.title: The Vocabulary
sections.content:
**Artificial intelligence** is the broad field: systems performing tasks that historically required human intelligence. **Machine learning** is the dominant approach within it: systems that learn patterns from data rather than following hand-written rules. **Generative AI** is the subset that produces new content — text, images, code — and **large language models (LLMs)** are its text engines, trained on vast corpora to predict likely continuations of language.

The hierarchy matters for precision: every LLM is generative AI, every generative model is (in practice) machine learning, and all of it is AI — but 'AI' in a vendor pitch can mean anything from a regression model to a chatbot.

sections.title: What They Do — and Get Wrong
sections.content:
Pattern-learning systems inherit their training data's blind spots: **bias** in the data becomes bias in the output, at scale and with a confident tone. Generative systems can **hallucinate** — produce fluent, plausible, *wrong* content, including invented facts, citations, and numbers. Neither failure announces itself; the output looks exactly like the correct kind.

The project-management consequence is a validation rule: AI output is a *draft* or a *signal*, never evidence. A generated risk analysis, status summary, or schedule suggestion is unverified until a human with context checks it — precisely because its errors are camouflaged as competence.

sections.title: Decision Support, Not Decision Maker
sections.content:
The governing frame: AI supports human decisions; it doesn't own them. A project manager remains accountable for every estimate, analysis, and communication the project produces — 'the model said so' transfers nothing. This holds even as capability grows: delegation of *tasks* to AI can deepen, but delegation of *accountability* cannot.

For exam purposes, options where AI decides autonomously (auto-rejecting change requests, auto-updating baselines, auto-communicating to stakeholders without review) are wrong regardless of efficiency framing.

sections.title: Exam Perspective
sections.content: Vocabulary questions test the hierarchy (LLMs ⊂ generative AI ⊂ ML ⊂ AI). Judgment questions test the two reflexes: validate before relying (hallucination and bias hide in fluent output), and keep accountability human (AI as instrument, never authority). Any option that treats AI output as self-certifying fact is a distractor.

exam_traps: Treating fluent AI output as verified fact — hallucinations look exactly like competence.
Letting 'the model recommended it' stand in for human accountability.
Assuming bias problems are rare edge cases rather than inherited defaults needing checks.

flashcards.definition: AI that produces new content — text, images, code — rather than only classifying or predicting.
Fluent, plausible, wrong output — invented facts or citations delivered with confidence.
AI's proper project role: informing human decisions, never owning them.

knowledge_checks.question: An AI assistant produces a polished risk analysis for the project, complete with statistics and citations. What must happen before it informs any decision?
options: Human validation of its claims — fluent output can contain invented facts and figures
Nothing further — statistical output from an AI system is inherently objective
Reformatting into the organization's template, which is the PM's only remaining task
A second AI system should cross-check the first one's analysis automatically
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Human validation of its claims — fluent output can contain invented facts and figures
knowledge_checks.explanation: Generative systems hallucinate — confident, well-formatted output can embed fabricated statistics and citations, and nothing in the polish signals the errors. Objectivity isn't conferred by being machine-generated, formatting isn't validation, and stacking a second unvalidated system compounds rather than solves the problem.

knowledge_checks.question: Which statement correctly orders the vocabulary?
options: Machine learning is a subset of large language models
AI and generative AI are unrelated fields that happen to share a name
Generative AI produces classification labels only, never new content
Large language models are generative AI, which is machine learning, which is AI
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Large language models are generative AI, which is machine learning, which is AI
knowledge_checks.explanation: The nesting runs LLM ⊂ generative AI ⊂ machine learning ⊂ AI. Reversing ML and LLMs inverts the hierarchy, generative systems produce content rather than only labels, and generative AI sits inside — not beside — the broader AI field.

knowledge_checks.question: A vendor proposes an AI feature that automatically approves or rejects low-value change requests 'to free up governance time.' What is the principled objection?
options: The threshold for 'low-value' is probably set incorrectly in the configuration
Approval is an accountable human decision — AI can analyze and recommend, not decide
The feature would be acceptable if it only auto-approved rather than auto-rejected
Governance time savings are too small to justify the licensing cost
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Approval is an accountable human decision — AI can analyze and recommend, not decide
knowledge_checks.explanation: Change decisions carry accountability that cannot transfer to a model — AI legitimately drafts the impact analysis and recommendation, but the decision stays human. Threshold tuning and cost arguments miss the principle, and auto-approving is no more delegable than auto-rejecting.

title: Automation, Assistance, and Augmentation
learning_objectives: Distinguish the three AI adoption levels by task complexity and human involvement.
Match project tasks to the appropriate adoption level.
Explain why oversight increases — not decreases — as the level rises.

sections.title: Three Levels
sections.content:
**Automation** hands AI repetitive, low-complexity, well-defined work: generating routine reports, organizing and categorizing information, meeting transcription and summaries, data entry. Output is checked, but the task itself needs little judgment.

**Assistance** pairs AI with human work that requires judgment: drafting documents the human revises, brainstorming risks or options, first-pass analysis the human refines. The human shapes and owns every output.

**Augmentation** applies AI to complex judgment work: scenario analysis, forecasting, multi-criteria trade-off exploration, pattern detection across large project datasets. AI extends what the human can consider — and human judgment matters *most* here, because the stakes and subtlety are highest.

sections.title: Matching Task to Level
sections.content:
The matching logic: how well-defined is the task, and how much judgment does it embed? Formatting a weekly status report from structured data → automation. Drafting a stakeholder communication about a sensitive delay → assistance (tone and relationship judgment stay human). Exploring how three staffing scenarios interact with schedule risk → augmentation (AI widens the analysis; the human weighs what the organization can absorb).

Mis-matching runs both directions: using judgment-level AI for rote work wastes capability, but pushing accountable judgment onto automation-level trust — auto-sending, auto-deciding — is the dangerous one.

sections.title: Oversight Scales Up
sections.content: Counterintuitively, higher adoption levels demand *more* human engagement, not less. Automation errors are usually visible and cheap (a misformatted report). Augmentation errors are subtle and expensive (a forecast quietly built on a biased assumption, steering a major decision). The oversight rule: the more consequential the judgment AI is informing, the more rigorous the human validation — check the inputs, challenge the assumptions, and never let the tool's confidence substitute for the organization's.

sections.title: Exam Perspective
sections.content: Classification questions give a task and ask the level — key on task complexity and judgment content, not tool branding. Judgment questions test the oversight rule: augmentation-level output feeding big decisions gets the most scrutiny, and any option where rising AI capability justifies shrinking human review is a distractor.

exam_traps: Auto-sending or auto-deciding — automation-level trust applied to judgment-level stakes.
Assuming more capable AI means less human oversight — the relationship runs the other way.
Classifying by tool sophistication instead of by the task's judgment content.

flashcards.definition: AI on repetitive, well-defined, low-judgment work — reports, organization, transcription.
AI drafting and analyzing alongside a human who shapes, revises, and owns the output.
AI extending complex judgment — scenarios, forecasts, trade-offs — where human validation matters most.

knowledge_checks.question: A PM uses AI to transcribe meetings and file the notes by topic; separately, AI explores how three staffing scenarios affect schedule risk for a major decision. Which levels are these?
options: Both are automation, since the same underlying model can perform each task
Assistance and automation respectively, based on which task came first
Transcription is augmentation because meetings involve human judgment
Automation (transcription and filing) and augmentation (scenario analysis)
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Automation (transcription and filing) and augmentation (scenario analysis)
knowledge_checks.explanation: Transcription and categorization are repetitive, well-defined work — automation. Multi-scenario risk exploration informs complex judgment — augmentation. The level follows the task's judgment content, not the model used or task order, and meetings containing judgment doesn't make transcribing them judgment work.

knowledge_checks.question: As an organization moves from automation toward augmentation uses of AI, what happens to the need for human oversight?
options: It increases — subtler, higher-stakes errors demand more rigorous validation
It decreases — more capable systems require proportionally less checking
It stays flat — oversight is a fixed compliance obligation at every level
It disappears once the system passes an initial certification review
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: It increases — subtler, higher-stakes errors demand more rigorous validation
knowledge_checks.explanation: Augmentation informs consequential judgments, and its failure modes (biased assumptions, plausible-but-wrong analysis) are harder to spot than a malformed report. Capability doesn't reduce the stakes; flat-compliance framing misses the scaling; and no certification makes ongoing validation unnecessary.

knowledge_checks.question: A team configures AI to draft AND automatically send weekly stakeholder updates 'since the drafts have been accurate lately.' What is the flaw?
options: Weekly cadence is wrong — stakeholder updates should always be daily instead
The drafts should be substantially longer before automation becomes appropriate
Communication embeds judgment and accountability — accuracy streaks don't make auto-send safe
Nothing — demonstrated accuracy is exactly how automation legitimately earns trust
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Communication embeds judgment and accountability — accuracy streaks don't make auto-send safe
knowledge_checks.explanation: Outbound stakeholder communication carries tone, relationship, and accountability judgment — assistance-level work where a human reviews before send. A streak of good drafts doesn't change the category: the first hallucinated or tone-deaf message goes straight to stakeholders. Cadence and length are beside the point.

title: Responsible AI and Human Accountability

eco_mappings.task: Task 2: Plan and manage project compliance

learning_objectives: Apply the responsible-AI checklist: oversight, accuracy, bias, privacy, security, transparency, copyright, sustainability.
Handle data protection when AI tools enter project workflows.
Keep accountability human in every AI-assisted decision path.

sections.title: The Non-Negotiables
sections.content: Responsible AI use in projects reduces to a checklist of standing obligations. **Human oversight**: a person reviews and owns consequential outputs. **Accuracy validation**: claims get verified before they inform decisions. **Bias and fairness**: outputs affecting people (resource assignments, evaluations, prioritizations touching groups differently) get checked for inherited bias. **Privacy and confidentiality**: sensitive data doesn't enter tools without authorization and safeguards. **Security**: AI tools are an attack and leakage surface, governed like any system handling project data. **Transparency**: where AI materially contributed to an analysis or decision, that's disclosable, not hidden. **Copyright and licensing**: generated content can carry IP risk, and feeding proprietary material into external tools can violate agreements. **Sustainability**: compute has real resource costs that belong in the environmental ledger.

sections.title: Data Discipline
sections.content: The most common project-level violation is casual data leakage: pasting confidential stakeholder information, unpublished financials, or a partner's proprietary material into an external AI tool 'to summarize it.' The discipline mirrors any data-handling rule: know the tool's data terms (is input retained? used for training?), use only approved tools for sensitive categories, and when in doubt, don't paste. A convenience summary is never worth a confidentiality breach — and the breach happens at paste time, not at output time.

sections.title: Accountability Cannot Transfer
sections.content:
Every responsible-AI principle folds back into one: a human owns the outcome. 'The AI screened the résumés,' 'the model flagged the vendor,' 'the tool wrote the risk assessment' — none of these sentences moves accountability anywhere. If an AI-assisted decision harms someone, misstates status, or violates an obligation, the accountability question is answered exactly as if a human staffer had drafted it: the person who relied on it owns it.

This is the same accountable-leadership principle from the mindset lessons, extended to a new class of instrument.

sections.title: Exam Perspective
sections.content: Scenario signals: sensitive data + external tool → stop at the paste; AI output affecting people → bias check before use; 'the model decided' → return the decision to its human owner; undisclosed AI contribution to a stakeholder-facing analysis → transparency failure. The credited option validates, discloses, protects data, and keeps a named human accountable.

exam_traps: Pasting confidential project or partner data into external AI tools for convenience.
Using AI outputs that affect people without checking for inherited bias.
Letting 'the model decided' obscure the human who owns the decision.

flashcards.definition: A person reviews and owns every consequential AI-assisted output — the anchor principle.
Sensitive information entering an external tool without authorization — the breach happens at input.
Disclosing where AI materially contributed to analyses or decisions others rely on.

knowledge_checks.question: A team member wants to paste a partner company's confidential technical specification into a public AI chatbot 'just to get a quick summary.' What is the responsible-AI ruling?
options: Stop — sensitive data entering an unapproved external tool is itself the breach
Proceed, since summarization is a low-risk, effectively read-only operation
Proceed as long as the summary is deleted immediately after being read once
Proceed if the chatbot's terms of service are carefully reviewed afterward
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Stop — sensitive data entering an unapproved external tool is itself the breach
knowledge_checks.explanation: The violation occurs at paste time: confidential partner material has left authorized custody, whatever happens next. Summarization intent, output deletion, and after-the-fact terms reading don't retrieve the data — the check happens before input, with approved tools for sensitive categories.

knowledge_checks.question: An AI tool ranks subcontractor bids, and the PM notices its training reflects historical decisions that favored certain vendor profiles. Before using the ranking, what does responsible AI require?
options: Nothing — historical data is the most objective basis a ranking can have
Using only the top-ranked bid, since deeper inspection would introduce human bias
Discarding AI from procurement entirely, since bias can never be assessed
A bias check — inherited historical skew must be examined before the ranking informs the decision
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: A bias check — inherited historical skew must be examined before the ranking informs the decision
knowledge_checks.explanation: Models learn the past, including its skew — an output affecting vendor selection needs its inherited bias examined, not presumed neutral. Historical data isn't objectivity, blind adoption of the top rank automates the skew, and wholesale abandonment overcorrects when examination is available.

knowledge_checks.question: A status analysis sent to the steering committee was substantially AI-generated, unreviewed, and contained a fabricated trend figure that influenced a funding decision. Who is accountable?
options: The AI vendor, since their model fabricated the figure
No one — tooling errors are a recognized cost of modern work
The project manager who relied on and transmitted the unvalidated analysis
The steering committee, for failing to independently verify what they read
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: The project manager who relied on and transmitted the unvalidated analysis
knowledge_checks.explanation: Accountability doesn't transfer to instruments: the person who adopted and sent the unverified output owns the consequence, exactly as if a staffer's unchecked draft had gone out. Vendor liability and committee diligence are separate questions — neither relocates the professional obligation to validate before relying.

title: AI Use Cases Across the Project Life Cycle
learning_objectives: Map high-value AI use cases to project processes and life-cycle stages.
Pair every use case with its validation requirement.
Evaluate proposed AI applications with the level + responsibility framework.

sections.title: Where AI Earns Its Keep
sections.content: Across the life cycle, the recurring high-value patterns: **information work** — meeting summaries, status report drafting, document organization, knowledge retrieval from project archives (automation/assistance); **analysis work** — schedule optimization suggestions, risk pattern detection across historical projects, forecast modeling, multi-criteria decision analysis (assistance/augmentation); **communication support** — drafting tailored stakeholder communications, sentiment signals from feedback channels (assistance — human review before anything external); and **monitoring** — real-time anomaly alerts on cost, schedule, and quality data streams (automation feeding human attention).

sections.title: Every Use Case Carries a Validation Clause
sections.content:
Each application pairs with a check: generated summaries → verified against the source before distribution; schedule suggestions → sanity-checked against constraints the model can't see (politics, contracts, people); risk patterns → treated as candidates for the register, not entries; sentiment signals → treated as prompts for human conversation, not verdicts on stakeholders; anomaly alerts → tuned and triaged by humans, because alert fatigue kills monitoring faster than missing alerts do.

The pattern is constant: AI widens the funnel; humans remain the filter.

sections.title: Evaluating a Proposed Use
sections.content:
A proposed AI application gets three questions. *What level is this?* (automation, assistance, augmentation — sets the oversight bar.) *What are the responsibility exposures?* (sensitive data in? biased output affecting people? IP in the output? disclosure owed?) *What's the validation loop?* (who checks, against what, before the output matters?) A use case that can't answer the third question isn't ready — efficiency claims don't substitute for a validation design.

This rubric also keeps adoption grounded in value: an AI feature that saves an hour of drafting but creates two hours of validation isn't automation, it's overhead.

sections.title: Exam Perspective
sections.content: Use-case questions reward matching applications to processes with the oversight intact: AI drafts the report, the PM verifies and owns it; AI flags the risk pattern, the team assesses it into (or out of) the register; AI suggests the schedule optimization, the human checks it against unmodeled reality. Distractors ship unvalidated output, skip the data-protection question, or promise oversight-free efficiency.

exam_traps: Adopting AI schedule or risk suggestions without checking constraints the model can't see.
Distributing generated summaries or communications without source verification.
Approving a use case with no designed validation loop because the efficiency story is good.

flashcards.definition: The designed human check every AI use case needs: who verifies, against what, before the output matters.
AI monitoring of data streams that feeds human attention — automation in service of judgment.
Untuned alerting that trains humans to ignore alerts — a monitoring failure mode AI can amplify.

knowledge_checks.question: AI analysis of historical projects flags three risk patterns that may apply to the current project. What is the credited handling?
options: Enter all three directly into the risk register with AI-estimated probabilities
Discard the analysis, since historical patterns rarely transfer between projects
Ask the tool to re-run the analysis until the patterns stabilize
Treat the three as candidates for team assessment before any enters the register
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Treat the three as candidates for team assessment before any enters the register
knowledge_checks.explanation: AI pattern detection widens the identification funnel — valuable input, but the team's contextual assessment decides what's real for this project. Direct entry with model-estimated numbers skips validation; discarding wastes a legitimate signal; and re-running until stable is ritual, not assessment.

knowledge_checks.question: An AI scheduling tool proposes a resequencing that shortens the critical path by three weeks. Before adopting it, the essential check is:
options: Whether the tool's vendor formally certifies the algorithm as PMBOK-aligned
Whether the sequence survives constraints the model can't see — contracts, politics, people
Whether the three weeks recovered exceed the project's total float across all paths
No check is needed — mathematical optimization is correct by construction
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Whether the sequence survives constraints the model can't see — contracts, politics, people
knowledge_checks.explanation: Optimizers work the modeled world; real schedules answer to unmodeled constraints — contractual sequencing, stakeholder commitments, who can actually do what when. That's the human sanity check. Vendor certification and float arithmetic don't address it, and 'correct by construction' is true only inside the model.

knowledge_checks.question: A proposed AI use case promises major reporting time savings but has no answer to 'who validates the output, against what, before it goes out?' What is the verdict?
options: Approve — the efficiency case is strong and validation can evolve later
Approve for external stakeholders first, since they're less familiar with the details
Not ready — a use case without a designed validation loop isn't adoptable, whatever it saves
Reject AI reporting permanently, since validation overhead always exceeds savings
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Not ready — a use case without a designed validation loop isn't adoptable, whatever it saves
knowledge_checks.explanation: The validation loop is a design requirement, not a later patch — unverified output going where it matters is how fabricated figures reach steering committees. 'External first' inverts the risk (less familiar audiences detect errors worse), and permanent rejection overcorrects: the fix is designing the loop, then adopting.
